keyAchievements.description: Because he sought to break Western traditions and establish new values, he earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer'.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Origins of Christian Morality and Rationalism
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to uncovering the origins of Christian morality and rationalism, arguing that rational things originated from irrationality and madness.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated 'Loving Life on Earth'
keyAchievements.description: Opposed idealistic dichotomy and advocated for loving life in reality.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Totalitarianism, Chauvinism, and Anti-Semitism
keyAchievements.description: Spread ideas criticizing totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Professor of Classical Philology at the University of Basel
keyAchievements.description: At age 24, he was appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland.

keyAchievements.title: Obtained Doctorate
keyAchievements.description: Received a doctorate from the University of Leipzig in 1869 based solely on his published writings.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'
keyAchievements.description: In 1872, he wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy', containing an original aesthetic theory about ancient Greek tragedy.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'
keyAchievements.description: Between 1873 and 1876, he wrote 'Untimely Meditations', containing cultural criticism that challenged the development of German culture.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Human, All Too Human'
keyAchievements.description: In 1878, he published 'Human, All Too Human', full of his characteristic aphorisms, beginning his departure from the philosophies of Schopenhauer and Wagner.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Death of God'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the 'Death of God', meaning the loss of the highest values created by humans and the advent of nihilism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Monism (Vital Ontology)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a monism contrary to the dualism of modern philosophy centered on the will to power, breaking away from the distinction between subject and object.

keyAchievements.title: Distinguished Concepts of Nihilism
keyAchievements.description: Divided nihilism into active and passive nihilism, emphasizing the importance of an attitude that creates meaning for oneself.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Idea of 'Eternal Recurrence'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the idea of 'eternal recurrence', inspired in Sils Maria in 1881, as the fundamental thought of 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Übermensch'
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the 'Übermensch', a human type who overcomes and creates by moving beyond modern values, morality, and faith.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Will to Power'
keyAchievements.description: As a concept opposing the instinct of self-preservation, he presented the 'will to power', arguing that the purpose of life is not survival but the exertion of power.

keyAchievements.title: Moral Criticism
keyAchievements.description: Classified morality into 'slave morality' and 'master morality', and criticized morality by examining its genealogy from psychological and axiological perspectives.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions as a Classical Philologist
keyAchievements.description: Influenced modern aesthetic theory based on a deep understanding of ancient Greece and Rome.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Posthumous Influence on European Philosophy and Literature
keyAchievements.description: Though ignored during his lifetime, he influenced numerous thinkers and writers posthumously, including Heidegger, Jaspers, Freud, and Thomas Mann.

keyAchievements.title: Composing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Composed lieder and piano pieces from about age 13 to 20, and left several compositions during his time in Basel.

faq.question: Why was Nietzsche called the 'philosopher with a hammer'?
faq.answer: He earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer' because he sought to shatter Western traditions and establish new values.

faq.question: What was the cause of Nietzsche's mental illness?
faq.answer: He suffered a mental breakdown in his later years; rumors that it was due to syphilis are just hearsay, and according to a Telegraph article, it was due to a malignant brain tumor.

faq.question: Was Nietzsche anti-Semitic?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but his book 'The Antichrist' explicitly contains content that provides a basis for hatred of Jews.

faq.question: Is Nietzsche's philosophy related to Nazism?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but after his death, his sister Elisabeth distorted his works to favor the Nazi regime. However, there are also points made that his officially published works were also utilized by the Nazis.

faq.question: What kind of evaluation did Nietzsche receive during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, he was thoroughly ignored by academia, harshly criticized by religious circles and moralists, and suffered from slander that he was corrupting society.

timeline.event: Born in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia).
Appointed Professor of Logic and Metaphysics at the University of Königsberg.
Published his groundbreaking work, 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals', introducing the categorical imperative.
Published 'Critique of Practical Reason', focusing on moral philosophy.
Published 'Critique of Judgment', focusing on aesthetics and teleology.
Died in Königsberg.
Obtained a professorship in philosophy at the University of Königsberg and published his inaugural dissertation.
Finished his work as a librarian at the Royal Library.
Published the first edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the essays 'Answering the Question: What Is Enlightenment?' and 'Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals'.
Published 'Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science' and was elected rector of the University of Königsberg until 1788.
Published the second edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Practical Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Judgment'.
Had a conflict with the faculty administration over the publication and censorship of the essay 'On the Radical Evil in Human Nature'.
Published 'Religion within the Boundaries of Mere Reason'.
Published 'Perpetual Peace: A Philosophical Sketch'.
Published 'The Metaphysics of Morals'.
Passed away at 4:00 AM on February 12 at the age of 80.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Pure Reason
keyAchievements.description: Revolutonized philosophy by attempting to resolve the conflict between rationalism and empiricism through a 'Copernican revolution' in epistemology.

keyAchievements.title: The Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the supreme principle of morality: to act only according to a maxim that one can simultaneously will to become a universal law.

keyAchievements.title: Transcendental Idealism
keyAchievements.description: Developed the doctrine that human experience of things is based on the way our minds structure them (phenomena), while things as they are in themselves (noumena) remain unknowable.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Practical Reason'
keyAchievements.description: Focused on ethics and found the basis of morality in transcendental freedom not governed by causality.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Judgment'
keyAchievements.description: Showcased the essence of critical philosophy by studying aesthetics, teleology, and more.

keyAchievements.title: Attempted a Copernican Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Pointed out the limits of human reason and attempted a 'Copernican Revolution' by introducing transcendental forms into human cognition.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Valued the motive more than the result of an action, presenting the categorical imperative as a command of duty that must be unconditionally followed in any situation.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Perpetual Peace
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a League of Nations based on the concept of international law to realize a perpetual peace without wars between countries.

faq.question: What did Kant mean by his 'Copernican revolution'?
faq.answer: Just as Copernicus argued that the sun, not the earth, is the center of the universe, Kant argued that instead of our knowledge conforming to objects, objects conform to our a priori forms of intuition (space and time) and categories of understanding.

faq.question: Is it true he never left his hometown?
faq.answer: Yes, it is a famous biographical fact that Kant never traveled more than 10 miles from his birthplace of Königsberg during his entire 79-year life, yet he became one of the most influential thinkers in global history.

faq.question: What is the categorical imperative in simple terms?
faq.answer: It is a moral rule stating that you should only take an action if you would be okay with everyone else in the world taking that same action in the same situation. It is essentially an ultimate standard of fairness and duty.

faq.question: What is unique about Kant's life?
faq.answer: Kant never traveled more than 100 miles away from Königsberg in his entire life and lived a highly regular daily routine.

timeline.event: Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born in Switzerland.
His father, Paul Jung, was appointed to a larger parish in Laufen (when Jung was 6).
Met Sigmund Freud for the first time in Vienna.
Began developing his analytical psychology theories independently.
Resigned from the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Served as president of the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Published the book 'Psychology of the Unconscious'.
Published the book 'A Psychological Approach to the Dogma of the Trinity'.
Published the book 'Transformation Symbolism in the Mass'.
Published the book 'Answer to Job'.
Passed away at the age of 85.
The book 'Memories, Dreams, Reflections' was published (posthumous publication).

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytical Psychology
keyAchievements.description: As a Swiss psychiatrist, he pursued an independent path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of Complex
keyAchievements.description: Used the word 'complex' to describe specific states and laid the foundation for the related theories.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of the Collective Unconscious
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the collective unconscious, a repository of mental materials that are part of the personal unconscious but shared through the history and culture of humanity rather than personal experience.

keyAchievements.title: Personality Classification (Introversion/Extraversion)
keyAchievements.description: Classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the Concept of Individuation
keyAchievements.description: Developed and theorized psychotherapy, viewing that individuals undergoing psychotherapy could advance into whole persons through the process of 'individuation'.

keyAchievements.title: Asserted the Self Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Asserted that the self archetype, which allows the unconscious within humans to realize a unified whole, possesses a transcendent function.

keyAchievements.title: Concepts of Anima and Animus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concepts of anima and animus, which represent the opposite gender in a person's psyche.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the archetype, a term borrowed from anthropology meaning universal human mental images or themes.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Persona
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of persona, an aspect of personality that appears for adaptation or personal convenience.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Shadow
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the shadow, which includes repressed and poorly known aspects of the personality often considered negative.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Self
keyAchievements.description: Viewed the 'self', a central concept governing the individuation process and symbolized by a mandala that unifies the feminine and masculine, as the main archetype of the psyche.

faq.question: Who is Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung was a Swiss psychiatrist, the founder of analytical psychology, who established the concepts of the complex and collective unconscious, and classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

faq.question: Why did Carl Jung and Freud part ways?
faq.answer: Freud restricted the concept of 'libido' to sexuality, while Jung saw it more broadly as general energy, which led to a divergence of views and their split.

faq.question: What is the field of psychology founded by Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung pursued his own path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

faq.question: How did Carl Jung speak about God?
faq.answer: He mentioned about God in a broadcast interview, saying, "I do not believe in Him, I know Him."

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Birth of Sun Tzu (Estimated)
Became a military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu
Launched an expedition against Chu with Helü and Wu Zixu as commanders, capturing the Chu capital, Ying
Despite Sun Tzu's opposition, Helü attacked Yue, was defeated, and died
Death of Sun Tzu (Estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Art of War
keyAchievements.description: As a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, he authored the 13 chapters of 'The Art of War'.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Military Strategist of Wu
keyAchievements.description: Became the military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu and trained the army.

keyAchievements.title: Victorious Expedition against Chu
keyAchievements.description: In 506 BC, contributed to the capture of the capital Ying by commanding the expedition against Chu with Helü.

keyAchievements.title: Assisted in the Great Victory over Yue
keyAchievements.description: Assisted Helü's successor, Fuchai, in building national strength and then attacking Yue, contributing to a great victory.

faq.question: Who is Sun Tzu?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu was a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, whose courtesy name was Changqing, better known by the honorific Sun Tzu. He wrote 'The Art of War'.

faq.question: Are the records of Sun Tzu's life clear?
faq.answer: There are important records like Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian, but his life is a subject of debate, and his life after retirement is unknown.

faq.question: How did Sun Tzu become a military strategist for Wu?
faq.answer: He fled to Wu with his clan, got to know the chancellor Wu Zixu, and was invited by King Helü of Wu to become a military strategist upon Wu Zixu's recommendation.

faq.question: What is Sun Tzu's representative work?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu's representative work is 'The Art of War', which was treated as an important military text in the Sinosphere for a long time.

timeline.event: Born in Chirnside, north of Edinburgh.
Changed his surname from Home to Hume, and worked as a clerk in Bristol for a few months.
At age 26, completed his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature'.
Applied for a professorship at the University of Edinburgh with essays on morals and politics but failed.
Served as a tutor to the Marquess of Annandale, and began writing 'The History of England'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals'.
Returned to Edinburgh and served as the Keeper of the Advocates' Library.
Published the 6-volume 'The History of England', written over 15 years.
Published 'Four Dissertations' and 'The Natural History of Religion'.
Published 'Sister Peg'.
Worked as secretary to the British Ambassador to France and formed a friendship with Rousseau.
Published 'My Own Life', died on August 25, and was buried in Edinburgh.
'Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion' was published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Role as a Philosopher, Economist, and Historian
keyAchievements.description: A Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian who is one of the leading figures associated with Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'A Treatise of Human Nature'
keyAchievements.description: At age 26, he wrote his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature', which is considered one of the most important books in the history of philosophy today.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The History of England'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'The History of England' over 15 years, publishing it in 6 volumes from 1754 to 1762.

keyAchievements.title: Raised the 'Is-Ought Problem'
keyAchievements.description: Raised the 'is-ought problem', stating that merely describing 'facts' cannot lead to normative conclusions about what 'ought' to be, establishing it as a core issue in modern metaethics.

keyAchievements.title: Presented 'Sympathy' as the Psychological Basis of Morality
keyAchievements.description: Viewed morality as a matter of feeling and established sympathy as the psychological foundation of morality, becoming a precursor to utilitarian moral theory.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated an Empirical Methodology for Human Sciences
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for human science as a science studied by the empirical method of 'experience and observation', attempting to analyze the basic facts of the human mind.

keyAchievements.title: Raised the Problem of Induction
keyAchievements.description: Raised the 'problem of induction', arguing that the reliability of inductive reasoning is not guaranteed.

keyAchievements.title: Skeptical Stance on Causality
keyAchievements.description: Doubted the problem of causality, viewing that causality is actually just a probability established inductively through habit.

keyAchievements.title: Clarified the Role of Goods in the Market Economy
keyAchievements.description: Argued that money (goods) is a medium of exchange in the market system, and that the amount of money holds no meaning in a society that has reached a state of equilibrium.

faq.question: Who was David Hume?
faq.answer: David Hume was a Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian, and one of the leading figures associated with Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Hume's philosophy?
faq.answer: Hume's philosophy is generally viewed as an extension of skepticism, and there is debate over whether naturalistic elements are treated as important.

faq.question: What was Hume's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Hume expressed his views on all religions, but because talking about religion was delicate at the time, his works concerning religion were published posthumously.

faq.question: What were Hume's political views?
faq.answer: Hume belonged to the skeptical and moderate Tory party, and emphasized the importance of the order of law and the public spirit for the community.

faq.question: How did Hume view the economy?
faq.answer: Hume believed that commerce and trade would raise people's living standards and bring cultural refinement, and that the dictatorial power of a government would weaken if the nation became affluent.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a country lawyer in Wrington, Somerset.
Entered Westminster School and graduated with excellent grades.
Entered Christ Church, Oxford, as a scholar and encountered Descartes' philosophy.
Received his Bachelor of Arts.
Began working as a tutor at Oxford University for 5 years.
Began studying natural sciences and medicine.
While staying in Brandenburg, Germany as an embassy secretary, witnessed the possibility of religious tolerance.
Appointed secretary to the Board of Trade and Plantations.
Began writing his major work, 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'.
Returned to England.
Fled to the Netherlands to escape the Royalists.
Returned to England following the accession of William III through the Glorious Revolution.
Became Commissioner of Appeals and became famous by publishing 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', which he wrote while in exile.
Retired to Essex.
Died in Essex.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of British Empiricism
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Social Contract Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the security of natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Enlightenment and Liberal Thinker
keyAchievements.description: Widely known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Concept of 'Self-Identity'
keyAchievements.description: The first philosopher to define the self through the continuity of 'consciousness'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the 'Blank Slate' (Tabula Rasa)
keyAchievements.description: Argued that people are born without innate ideas, viewing the mind as a 'blank slate'.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Night-Watchman State Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued for a night-watchman state, stating that the state's duty lies in ensuring a minimum level of security.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for Popular Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for popular sovereignty by arguing that the people entrust power to the state through a contract.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Constitutional Democracy and Natural Rights
keyAchievements.description: The first person to advocate for constitutional democracy and natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for the Separation of Powers
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for the separation of powers among the legislative, judicial, and executive branches.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'
keyAchievements.description: His major work dealing with the problem of cognition, including modern science.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Reasonableness of Christianity'
keyAchievements.description: A text written as a response to John Toland's draft.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'
keyAchievements.description: Argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church'.

faq.question: What kind of philosopher is John Locke considered to be?
faq.answer: Locke is evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher, and is known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

faq.question: What are John Locke's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', 'Two Treatises of Government', 'The Reasonableness of Christianity', and 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'.

faq.question: Who did John Locke's ideas influence?
faq.answer: Locke's ideas influenced Voltaire, Rousseau, the American Revolution, and Scottish Enlightenment thinkers, and are also reflected in the United States Declaration of Independence.

faq.question: What is the 'blank slate' advocated by John Locke?
faq.answer: Locke viewed the mind as a 'blank slate' (tabula rasa), arguing that people are born without innate ideas.

faq.question: What does John Locke's social contract theory argue?
faq.answer: Locke argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the safety of natural rights, including holding one's own property through labor in a peaceful state of nature.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on toleration?
faq.answer: Locke argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church', but he showed a contradictory attitude by excluding Roman Catholics or atheists from being objects of toleration.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Locke showed a contradictory attitude by opposing slavery in England while accepting the ownership of black slaves by English people in the American colonies.

timeline.event: Simone de Beauvoir is born in Paris, France.
Presents on Leibniz at the Sorbonne and passes the agrégation in philosophy in second place.
Publishes the novel She Came to Stay (L'Invitée).
Writes Pyrrhus and Cineas (Pyrrhus et Cinéas), an essay on existentialist ethics.
Publishes The Ethics of Ambiguity (Pour Une Morale de L'ambiguïté).
The Second Sex (Le Deuxième Sexe) is published in France.
Publishes the autobiography Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter (Mémoires d'une jeune fille rangée).
Publishes the autobiography The Prime of Life (La Force de l'âge).
Publishes the autobiography Force of Circumstance (La Force des choses).
Writes the essay Old Age (La Vieillesse).
Signs the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.
Publishes the autobiography All Said and Done (Tout compte fait).
Publishes When Things of the Spirit Come First (Quand prime le spirituel), containing stories of her experiences as a woman in her youth.
Writes Adieux: A Farewell to Sartre (La Cérémonie Des Adieux), covering Sartre's later years.
Dies of pneumonia and is buried in the Montparnasse Cemetery in Paris.

keyAchievements.title: Writer and Philosopher
keyAchievements.description: A prominent French writer and philosopher who wrote in various fields including novels, philosophical essays, biographies, and autobiographies.

keyAchievements.title: She Came to Stay
keyAchievements.description: A novelized chronicle of the complex relationship between herself, Sartre, Olga Kosakiewicz, and Wanda Kosakiewicz.

keyAchievements.title: The Mandarins
keyAchievements.description: A Goncourt Prize-winning novel set after World War II, depicting surrounding figures like Sartre and Nelson Algren.

keyAchievements.title: The Second Sex
keyAchievements.description: A monumental work containing an in-depth analysis of women's oppression, becoming a cornerstone of modern feminism.

keyAchievements.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
keyAchievements.description: An accessible introduction to French existentialism, possessing a simplicity that contrasts with Sartre's more difficult works.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Editing of Les Temps Modernes
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the political magazine Les Temps Modernes with Sartre and Merleau-Ponty, and worked as an editor until shortly before her death.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Autobiographies
keyAchievements.description: Documented her life through four famous autobiographies, beginning with Memoirs of a Dutiful Daughter.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the French Women's Liberation Movement
keyAchievements.description: Actively participated in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 advocating for the legalization of abortion.

faq.question: Who was Simone de Beauvoir?
faq.answer: She was a French writer and philosopher who wrote novels, as well as essays, biographies, and autobiographies on philosophy, politics, and social issues.

faq.question: What are her major works?
faq.answer: She wrote metaphysical novels such as She Came to Stay and The Mandarins, and in 1949 wrote The Second Sex, which became a cornerstone of modern feminism.

faq.question: What was Simone de Beauvoir's family background?
faq.answer: She was born to Georges de Beauvoir, a legal secretary, and Françoise Brasseur, and her younger sister Hélène de Beauvoir was a painter.

faq.question: What was her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre?
faq.answer: She met Sartre at the University of Paris. In the 1929 philosophy agrégation, Sartre ranked first and she ranked second, and they maintained a complex lifelong relationship.

faq.question: What was the core argument in The Second Sex?
faq.answer: She argued that men had imposed a false aura of 'mystery' on women, turning them into the social 'Other', and adopting the maxim 'existence precedes essence', she posited that one is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

faq.question: How did she participate in the women's liberation movement?
faq.answer: She was active in the French women's liberation movement in the 1970s and signed the Manifesto of the 343 in 1971, demanding the legalization of abortion, which was illegal in France at the time.

timeline.event: Born in Linden, Germany.
Publishes dissertation on the concept of love.
Barred from obtaining a habilitation due to being Jewish and flees from Germany to Paris.
Marries Heinrich Blücher.
Flees to the United States with her husband and mother.
Becomes a naturalized US citizen.
Publishes The Origins of Totalitarianism.
Publishes Rahel Varnhagen: The Life of a Jewess and The Human Condition.
Becomes the first woman to be appointed to a full professorship at Princeton University.
Publishes Between Past and Future.
Publishes Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil and On Revolution.
Publishes Men in Dark Times.
Publishes Crises of the Republic: Lying in Politics, Civil Disobedience, On Violence.
Publishes the anthology Crises of the Republic.
Dies.
Her unfinished posthumous work, The Life of the Mind, is published.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing Identity as a Political Theorist
keyAchievements.description: Described herself as a political theorist rather than a philosopher, leaving achievements regarding humanity's habitation of the world.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Power, Politics, Authority, and Totalitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Left significant works on the nature of power and topics such as politics, authority, and totalitarianism.

keyAchievements.title: Theorizing the Concept of Freedom
keyAchievements.description: Theorized freedom as public and associative, focusing on affirming freedom as collective political action.

keyAchievements.title: Introducing the Concept of the 'Banality of Evil'
keyAchievements.description: In her report on the Eichmann trial, she proposed the conclusion that sheer thoughtlessness ultimately begets the banality of evil.

keyAchievements.title: Writing The Origins of Totalitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a book tracing the roots of Communism and Nazism and their connection to antisemitism.

keyAchievements.title: Writing On Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Compared the American Revolution and the French Revolution, arguing for the success of the American Revolution and the disaster of the French Revolution.

keyAchievements.title: Establishing the Theory of Power
keyAchievements.description: Defined power as something that 'cannot be the property of an individual, and exists only as long as the group remains together', strictly contrasting it with violence.

faq.question: How did Hannah Arendt define herself?
faq.answer: Arendt herself rejected the title of philosopher on the grounds that philosophy focuses on 'man in the singular', instead describing herself as a political theorist.

faq.question: What is the 'banality of evil' proposed by Arendt?
faq.answer: In her report on the Eichmann trial, Arendt concluded that sheer thoughtlessness ultimately gives rise to the banality of evil.

faq.question: What is Arendt's concept of power?
faq.answer: Arendt defined power as something that 'cannot be the property of an individual, and exists only as long as the group remains together', and strictly contrasted it with violence.

faq.question: How did Arendt evaluate the American and French Revolutions?
faq.answer: Arendt argued that the well-studied and often imitated French Revolution was a disaster, while the largely ignored American Revolution was a success.

timeline.event: Born in Copenhagen, Denmark.
Enrolls in the Faculty of Theology at the University of Copenhagen at age 18.
Mother, Ane Sørensdatter Lund Kierkegaard, dies.
Experiences an attempted suicide.
Meets Regine Olsen.
Father, Michael Pedersen Kierkegaard, dies.
Proposes to Regine Olsen.
Breaks off his engagement to Regine Olsen.
Graduates with a Ph.D. in philosophy from the University of Copenhagen.
Publishes his university dissertation, On the Concept of Irony with Continual Reference to Socrates.
Completes writing Either/Or.
Publishes Either/Or.
Publishes Fear and Trembling.
Publishes Repetition.
Publishes Philosophical Fragments.
Publishes The Concept of Anxiety.
Publishes Stages on Life's Way.
Peder Ludvig Møller publishes a critique of Stages on Life's Way.
Publishes Concluding Unscientific Postscript to Philosophical Fragments.
Publishes Works of Love.
Begins his attack on the Church of Denmark through works like Practice in Christianity.
Publishes The Sickness Unto Death.
In his later years, persistently criticizes the Danish Church.
Dies of spinal tuberculosis at Frederik's Hospital.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Existentialist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as a pioneer of existentialist philosophy for providing deep insights into individual existential choices and emotions.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Hegelian Idealism and the Danish Lutheran Church
keyAchievements.description: Developed his independent thoughts in opposition to the dominant Hegelian idealism and the formalism of the Danish Lutheran Church of his time.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Concepts of 'Subjectivity' and the 'Leap of Faith'
keyAchievements.description: Explored the essence of religious existence through the core concepts of human 'subjectivity' (or individuality) and the reason-transcending 'leap of faith'.

keyAchievements.title: Use of Indirect Communication and Pseudonyms
keyAchievements.description: Used various pseudonyms and indirect communication methods to construct complex dialogues, encouraging readers to find meaning themselves.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Christian Psychology
keyAchievements.description: Called the founder of Christian psychology for his in-depth analysis of human anxiety and despair, influencing existential psychology.

faq.question: How is Kierkegaard evaluated as a philosopher?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as a pioneer of existentialist philosophy, and in particular, he is classified as a 'Christian existentialist', distinct from atheistic existentialists.

faq.question: What are Kierkegaard's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: His two most widely known ideas are the concepts of the 'leap of faith' and 'subjectivity', which explain how individuals believe in God or act with love.

faq.question: Why did Kierkegaard use pseudonyms?
faq.answer: It was to encourage readers not to merely read the surface meaning of the text but to find the meaning themselves, and to prevent them from treating his ideas as an authoritative system.

faq.question: What was Kierkegaard's stance on the Church of Denmark?
faq.answer: In his later years, he criticized the Church of Denmark for distorting the true meaning of Christianity and becoming corrupt, pointing out the church's meaningless gatherings and bureaucratic tasks.

timeline.event: Born in Danzig as the eldest son of a merchant.
When Danzig was annexed by Prussia, his family moved to Hamburg.
His younger sister Adele was born, and he learned French for two years in Le Havre, France.
Upon returning from France, he enrolled in Dr. Runge's private school, a merchant training institution, and studied there for four years.
Traveled with his father to Hanover, Carlsbad, Prague, and Dresden.
At his father's suggestion, the whole family traveled across Europe, and he learned English in London.
Traveled through France, Switzerland, Vienna, Dresden, and Berlin, and began his merchant apprenticeship in Danzig.
His father died after falling from a warehouse ventilation window.
Following his father's death, the family moved to Weimar, but Schopenhauer remained in Hamburg to continue his merchant apprenticeship.
On his mother's advice, he abandoned his merchant apprenticeship and enrolled at the Gymnasium in Gotha to learn Latin and Greek.
Watched a play hosted by Napoleon in Erfurt and criticized the aristocratic female audience.
Enrolled in the Faculty of Medicine at the University of Göttingen but developed a stronger interest in philosophy.
Attended lectures by philosopher Gottlob Ernst Schulze and was advised to study Plato and Kant deeply.
Encouraged by Christoph Wieland, he decided to study philosophy and transferred to the University of Berlin.
Began a serious study of various thinkers, including Plato, Kant, Bacon, Locke, and Hume.

Completed his doctoral dissertation, On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, and earned his Ph.D. in philosophy. He presented the dissertation to Johann Wolfgang von Goethe and studied the theory of colors.
Read and explored the Oupnek'hat, a Latin translation of the Upanishads, at the Weimar Public Library, and experienced severe conflict with his mother.
Published On Vision and Colors based on his interactions with Goethe, criticizing both Newton's and Goethe's theories of color.
Completed and published his life's masterpiece, The World as Will and Representation, and traveled to Italy.
Applied for a lecturing position at the University of Berlin and returned home to help his mother when she faced bankruptcy.
Passed his trial lecture at the University of Berlin, but due to lack of popularity, his lectures ended after one semester.
Traveled to Italy, experiencing and documenting its culture, arts, and environment.
Returned to Germany and spent a depressed period suffering from various illnesses and hearing loss.
Stayed in Gastein, Mannheim, and Dresden, planning a translation of David Hume's work.
Returned to Berlin to learn Spanish and translated a Spanish book.
As cholera spread in Berlin, he moved to Frankfurt, where he spent the rest of his life.
Settled in Frankfurt, and his writings began to attract public attention.
Submitted a proposal for the plan to build a Goethe monument in Frankfurt, but it was rejected.
Published On the Will in Nature, reflecting his view that natural science aligned with his theory.

Intervened in the publication of Kant's complete works, recommending an emphasis on the A edition of the Critique of Pure Reason, and decided to enter a prize essay competition held by the Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences and Letters.
His mother, Johanna Schopenhauer, died, and he decided to enter a prize essay competition held by the Royal Danish Academy of Sciences and Letters.
Won the prize from the Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences and Letters for his essay On the Freedom of the Will.
Submitted his essay On the Basis of Morality to the Royal Danish Academy but failed to win the prize because he criticized Hegel and others.
Published the two prize essays together as The Two Fundamental Problems of Ethics.
Met his sister Adele for the first time in 20 years.
Completed the second edition of The World as Will and Representation and published it alongside a reprint of the first edition.
Began writing Parerga and Paralipomena.
Julius Frauenstädt met Schopenhauer and became his disciple.
Published a revised edition of On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason.
His sister Adele died.
Published Parerga and Paralipomena after years of writing; it became his most popular book.
English translator John Oxenford introduced his philosophy in the Westminster Review, bringing him to the attention of the British public.
Published a revised edition of On the Will in Nature, and Richard Wagner sent him a dedicated copy of The Ring of the Nibelung.
The philosophy department at Leipzig University posed a prize essay topic titled 'Elucidation and Critique of the Principles of Schopenhauer's Philosophy'.
Lectures on Schopenhauer were established at the Universities of Bonn and Breslau, and some of his books were translated in England and France.
His 70th birthday party was held, and he declined an offer to be elected to the Royal Prussian Academy of Sciences in Berlin.
The third edition of The World as Will and Representation was published.
Died of pneumonia at his home in Frankfurt on September 21.

keyAchievements.title: Writing On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason
keyAchievements.description: Wrote his doctoral dissertation, which became a classic in philosophy (epistemology).

keyAchievements.title: Publication of The World as Will and Representation
keyAchievements.description: Published his major philosophical work, regarded as his life's masterpiece.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of Eastern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Was the first in the West to introduce readers to the refinements of Eastern philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Atheism
keyAchievements.description: Is counted among the original philosophers who explicitly declared themselves atheists.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Parerga and Paralipomena
keyAchievements.description: Published a collection of essays on life in general, which made him a celebrity.

keyAchievements.title: Award from the Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences and Letters
keyAchievements.description: Won a prize from the Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences and Letters for his essay On the Freedom of the Will.

keyAchievements.title: Proposing Compassion as the Basis of Ethics
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Kant, he proposed compassion as the foundation of morality and explained it metaphysically.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Kantian Ethics
keyAchievements.description: Pointed out the weaknesses in Kant's nomological-deontological ethics, influencing modern ethical discourse.

faq.question: Which philosophers did Schopenhauer oppose?
faq.answer: He firmly believed that he correctly inherited Kant's philosophy and criticized Hegel, Fichte, Schelling, and others as pseudotheorists who distorted Kant's ideas.

faq.question: How did Schopenhauer learn about Eastern philosophy?
faq.answer: Through the orientalist Friedrich Majer in the 1820s, he learned about Hinduism and Buddhism, realizing that the core tenets of these religions were the same as his and Kant's conclusions.

faq.question: What are Schopenhauer's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include his doctoral dissertation, On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, and his life's masterpiece, The World as Will and Representation.

faq.question: Why did Schopenhauer continue his research outside the university?
faq.answer: After clashing with Hegel during his university lectures, he despised the cliques of university professors and continued his independent research without being tied to any institution.

faq.question: How did Schopenhauer evaluate Kant's philosophy?
faq.answer: While accepting Kant's ideas critically, he was convinced that he correctly inherited Kant's philosophy, and he praised Kant's discovery of the a priori nature of cognition as a great historical achievement.

faq.question: What did Schopenhauer see as the basis of ethics?
faq.answer: Unlike Kant, who saw reason and law as the foundation of morality, Schopenhauer proposed compassion as the basis of morality and explained it metaphysically.

timeline.event: Michelangelo di Lodovico Buonarroti Simoni is born.
Enters Domenico Ghirlandaio's workshop as an apprentice.
Completes the Pietà, commissioned by French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères.
Begins sculpting David, commissioned by the city of Florence.
Invited to Rome by Pope Julius II and commissioned to build the Pope's tomb.
Commissioned to paint the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel in the Apostolic Palace and begins the work.
Completes the Sistine Chapel ceiling.
Leaves Florence permanently and settles in Rome.
Commissioned by Pope Paul III to paint The Last Judgment on the altar wall of the Sistine Chapel.
Completes The Last Judgment.
Completes the statue of Moses for the tomb of Julius II.
Completes the fresco The Conversion of Saul in the Pauline Chapel, Vatican.
Completes the fresco The Crucifixion of St. Peter in the Pauline Chapel, Vatican.
Dies in Rome at the age of 89.

keyAchievements.title: Pietà
keyAchievements.description: A masterpiece of Renaissance sculptural art completed in Rome in 1499, commissioned by the French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères.

keyAchievements.title: David
keyAchievements.description: A statue of the young David, commissioned by the city of Florence in 1501 and completed over three years.

keyAchievements.title: Sistine Chapel Ceiling
keyAchievements.description: The ceiling fresco of the Apostolic Palace, commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1508 and completed in four years, depicting much of Catholic doctrine including the Creation.

keyAchievements.title: The Last Judgment
keyAchievements.description: The large altar wall fresco in the Sistine Chapel, commissioned by Pope Paul III in 1535 and completed over six years.

keyAchievements.title: Tomb of Julius II
keyAchievements.description: A funerary monument commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1505 and completed over 40 years, most famous for its statue of Moses.

faq.question: In what fields of art was Michelangelo active?
faq.answer: He was an Italian sculptor, painter, architect, and poet, a master who represented the Renaissance period.

faq.question: What are Michelangelo's representative sculptural works?
faq.answer: His representative sculptures include the Pietà completed in 1499 and the David completed over three years beginning in 1501.

faq.question: Who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling?
faq.answer: Michelangelo painted it, completing it in four years after being commissioned by Pope Julius II in 1508.

faq.question: Where is Michelangelo's The Last Judgment located?
faq.answer: This work, commissioned by Pope Paul III in 1535 and completed over six years, is the large altar wall fresco in the Sistine Chapel.

timeline.event: Claude Monet is born on November 14 in the 9th arrondissement of Paris.
Baptized Oscar-Claude on May 20 at the Notre-Dame de Lorette.
His family moves to Le Havre in Normandy.
Enrolls in the Le Havre secondary school of the arts on April 1.
His mother dies.
Meets fellow painter Eugène Boudin.
Arrives in Paris in April and settles at the Hotel 'du Nouveau Monde'.
Enrolls in the Académie Suisse, run by Charles Suisse, early in the year (around February) and meets Camille Pissarro.
Drafted into the army in Le Havre on March 2 and joins the First Regiment of African Light Cavalry on April 29.
Contracts typhoid fever in the spring, returns to Le Havre to recover, and meets Johan Jongkind in the summer.
Returns to Paris in December, enrolls in the studio of Charles Gleyre, and meets Auguste Renoir and Frédéric Bazille.
Successfully debuts at the Salon with The Point of la Hève at Low Tide and Mouth of the Seine at Honfleur.
Submits The Woman in the Green Dress and Pavé de Chailly to the Salon and is accepted.
His then-mistress Camille Doncieux gives birth to their first son, Jean.
Marries Camille on June 28, and takes refuge in London and the Netherlands just before the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War.
His father dies, and the family moves to Argenteuil.
Moves to the 'pink house with green shutters' in Argenteuil, and exhibits Impression, Sunrise at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
Paints Woman with a Parasol - Madame Monet and Her Son.
Camille Monet falls seriously ill; another exhibition is organized in opposition to the Salon.
Exhibits seven paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare at the Third Impressionist Exhibition on April 5.
His second son Michel is born, the family moves to the village of Vétheuil, and Camille is diagnosed with uterine cancer.
Camille dies.
Submits two paintings to the Salon, and one is accepted.
Moves to Poissy in December with Alice Hoschedé, the wife of Ernest Hoschedé, and her children.
Participates in his last, the Seventh Impressionist Exhibition.
Moves to a rented house in Giverny with the children on April 29, and his first major retrospective is held.
Goes on a short painting trip to the Italian Riviera and Genoa with Auguste Renoir in December.
Stays in Bordighera from January 18 to April 5, producing 38 paintings.
Travels to the Netherlands to paint tulips.
Meets John Singer Sargent in the summer.
Purchases the house in Giverny.
15 paintings of the Haystacks series are exhibited at the Galerie Durand-Ruel.
Paints 26 views of Rouen Cathedral.
Exhibits 20 paintings of Rouen Cathedral.
61 paintings are exhibited at the Galerie Georges Petit.
Begins painting water lilies, and stays in London for six weeks.
Exhibits the first garden paintings, primarily focusing on the Japanese bridge.
Expands the pond at his home; in February, an exhibition of the Water Lilies series in New York City achieves great success.
Increases the size of the water garden to nearly 4,000 square meters.
The series 'Views of the Thames in London - 1900-1904' is exhibited in May and June.
Spends the autumn at the Palazzo Barbaro in Venice with Alice.
An exhibition titled 'Water Lilies' opens on May 6, featuring 48 paintings.
His second wife Alice dies.
Travels to London to consult with the German ophthalmologist Richard Liebreich.
His eldest son Jean dies.
Begins work on a series of decorative paintings for the French government.
Begins a series of landscape paintings 'at full strength'.
Prescription for mydriatic drops temporarily alleviates his vision problems.
Undergoes cataract surgery.
With improved vision, begins retouching some of the works produced before surgery.
Dies of lung cancer at the age of 86 on December 5 and is buried in the Giverny church cemetery.
27 panel paintings are exhibited at the Musée de l'Orangerie in May.
André Masson's essay helps shift the perception of Monet's paintings.
Water Lily paintings held by the Museum of Modern Art in New York are destroyed in a fire.
Monet's house, garden, and water lily pond are bequeathed by Michel to the French Academy of Fine Arts.
Monet's garden is restored and opened to the public.
A Google Doodle is created on November 14 to commemorate Claude Monet's 161st birthday.
The Houses of Parliament, Sunlight Effect in the Fog is sold for $20.1 million.
A paper is published in the Proceedings of the Royal Society identifying the locations where the London paintings were created.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is stolen for the second time in August.
The Railway Bridge at Argenteuil is sold for $41.4 million on May 6.
Cliffs Near Dieppe is recovered in June.
Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas is sold for £40,921,250 (approx. $80,451,178) on June 24.
The Church at Vétheuil and Le Bassin Aux Nymphéas become the subject of a court case in New York in October.
A Monet painting formerly owned by a Jewish retailer is discovered in the suitcase of Cornelius Gurlitt.
The Nazi-looted artwork On the Seashore is restituted to the granddaughters of the Parlagi couple.

keyAchievements.title: Founding and Pioneering Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'father of Impressionism', he is considered a founder and pioneer of the movement and a key precursor to Modernism.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Term Impressionism
keyAchievements.description: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Consistent Practice of En Plein Air Landscape Painting
keyAchievements.description: As the most consistent practitioner of the Impressionist philosophy over a long career, he expressed his perception of nature in plein air landscape paintings.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering the Series Method
keyAchievements.description: Pioneered the series method of painting the same scene many times to capture changes in light and the passing of seasons, with major works including the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and Water Lilies series.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Giverny Garden
keyAchievements.description: Purchased a house and land in Giverny, northern France, and began a vast landscaping project including a water lily pond, which became the greatest source of inspiration for his art.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on Modern Art
keyAchievements.description: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration for 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

faq.question: Who was Claude Monet?
faq.answer: Claude Monet was a French Impressionist painter, a founder and pioneer of the Impressionist movement, often referred to as the 'father of Impressionism'.

faq.question: Where did the term 'Impressionism' originate?
faq.answer: The term 'Impressionism' originated from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited at the First Impressionist Exhibition in 1874.

faq.question: What are Monet's representative series paintings?
faq.answer: His representative series include the Haystacks, Rouen Cathedral, and the Water Lilies, which captivated him for the last 20 years of his life.

faq.question: Where was Monet primarily active?
faq.answer: He lived in Giverny, northern France, from 1883, where he purchased a house and land and began a vast landscaping project that included his water lily pond.

faq.question: How did Monet's eyesight affect his work?
faq.answer: Cataracts made color perception increasingly difficult, leading his paintings to become broader and more abstract; after surgery, his paintings tended to have stronger blue tones than before.

faq.question: How did Monet's work influence modern art?
faq.answer: His late works produced in Giverny under the influence of cataracts are considered to have provided 'major' inspiration to 20th-century art and modern abstract art.

timeline.event: Born into an artisan family in the Katsushika district of Edo (Childhood name: Tokitaro).
Began drawing around age 6.
Worked at a bookstore and lending library at age 12.
Worked as an apprentice woodcarver at age 14.
Entered the studio of Katsukawa Shunshō at age 18.
Received the name Shunrō from his master and published his first series of prints of Kabuki actors.
Produced Fireworks at Ryōgoku Bridge in Edo.
Death of his master Katsukawa Shunshō.
Second marriage.
Passed the name Tawaraya Sōri to his pupil, began working as an independent artist, and used the name "Hokusai Tomisa".
Began using the name "Katsushika Hokusai", published the landscape print series Famous Views of the Eastern Capital and Eight Views of Edo, and started taking on pupils.
Painted a massive portrait of the Buddhist monk Daruma, about 200 square meters in size, during an Edo festival.
Collaborated with popular novelist Kyokutei Bakin to produce a series of illustrated novels.
Produced illustrations for the fantasy novel Strange Tales of the Crescent Moon.
Changed his name to Taito at age 51 and began producing Hokusai Manga and various etehon (art manuals).
Produced Quick Lessons in Simplified Drawing.
The first volume of Hokusai Manga was published and became a huge success.
Painted a giant portrait of Daruma outside the Hongan-ji Nagoya Betsuin in Nagoya.
Changed his name to Iitsu and began gaining a solid reputation as an artist throughout Japan.
Produced his masterpiece, Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced Oceans of Wisdom (Shika Shashin-kyō).
Began working under the name "Gakyō Rōjin Manji" (The Old Man Mad About Art) and produced One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji.
Produced his last print series, One Hundred Poems Explained by the Nurse.
A fire destroyed his studio and much of his work.
Visited Obuse in Shinano Province at age 83 and stayed there for several years.
Painted Chinese lions (shishi) every morning as a talisman to ward off bad luck.
Painted Dragon Flying in Clouds above Mount Fuji and Tiger in Snow.
Died and was buried at Seikyō-ji Temple in Taito Ward, Tokyo.

keyAchievements.title: Production of Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced this ukiyo-e series, which includes the iconic The Great Wave off Kanagawa, earning immense fame both domestically and internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Expanding Ukiyo-e Subjects
keyAchievements.description: He played a crucial role in broadening the subjects of ukiyo-e, which had primarily focused on courtesans and actors, to include landscapes, plants, and animals.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Output
keyAchievements.description: Working tirelessly from childhood until his death, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, woodblock prints, and images for picture books.

keyAchievements.title: Innovative Composition and Depiction Skills
keyAchievements.description: He is considered one of the greatest masters in art history due to his innovative compositions and outstanding depictive skills.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Hokusai Manga
keyAchievements.description: He produced Hokusai Manga, containing thousands of drawings, which immediately became a huge success and was also used as a drawing manual.

keyAchievements.title: Experimentation with Western Perspective
keyAchievements.description: Among Japanese artists, he early on experimented with Western perspective, applying techniques to express depth and volume in his works.

keyAchievements.title: Influence on European Art
keyAchievements.description: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

keyAchievements.title: Production of One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji
keyAchievements.description: He produced another important series, One Hundred Views of Mount Fuji, evaluated as "the greatest masterpiece among his landscape collections."

faq.question: Who was Katsushika Hokusai?
faq.answer: He was a Japanese ukiyo-e painter and printmaker of the Edo period, who played a crucial role in expanding the subjects of ukiyo-e to landscapes, plants, and animals.

faq.question: What is Hokusai's most famous work?
faq.answer: The Great Wave off Kanagawa, included in his ukiyo-e series Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, is his most iconic work.

faq.question: What influence did Hokusai have on Western art?
faq.answer: Amid the wave of Japonisme that swept across Europe in the late 19th century, he greatly influenced Impressionist painters such as Vincent van Gogh and Claude Monet.

faq.question: How many names did Hokusai use in his lifetime?
faq.answer: He is known to have used at least 30 names during his lifetime, closely linked to changes in his artwork and style.

faq.question: When did Hokusai die?
faq.answer: He died on May 10, 1849, showing a passion for creating better works even on his deathbed.

timeline.event: Born in Torquay, Devon, England.
Went to Paris at age 16 to study singing and piano.
Married her first husband, Archibald Christie, a colonel in the Royal Flying Corps.
Worked in a hospital dispensary during World War I.
Her only daughter, Rosalind Hicks, was born.
Disappeared for 10 days on December 8, attracting media attention.
Remarried to archaeologist Max Mallowan.
Received the Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE).
Received the Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire (DBE).
Died at the age of 85.

keyAchievements.title: Reputation as a Mystery Writer
keyAchievements.description: Nicknamed the "Queen of Crime", she wrote over 80 mystery novels and gained immense popularity by creating the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

keyAchievements.title: Global Sales Records
keyAchievements.description: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in English and another 1 billion copies translated into 103 other languages.

keyAchievements.title: Most Translated Author
keyAchievements.description: She is recorded as the most translated author in the world by the Index Translationum.

keyAchievements.title: Longest-Running Play
keyAchievements.description: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

faq.question: What was Agatha Christie's full name?
faq.answer: Her full name was Dame Agatha Christie, Lady Mallowan, DBE.

faq.question: What pen name did Agatha Christie use?
faq.answer: She used the pen name Mary Westmacott when writing romance novels.

faq.question: Who are the famous detective characters created by Agatha Christie?
faq.answer: She created the great Belgian detective Hercule Poirot and Miss Marple.

faq.question: How many copies of Agatha Christie's works have been sold?
faq.answer: Her works are listed in the Guinness World Records, having sold over 1 billion copies in the English-speaking world and another 1 billion copies in editions translated into 103 languages.

faq.question: What is Agatha Christie's most famous play, and how long was it performed?
faq.answer: Her play The Mousetrap ran from its premiere in London in 1955 until November 2012.

timeline.event: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley was born.
Lost her mother just 11 days after birth.
Began a romance with Percy Bysshe Shelley, one of her father's political followers.
Conceived the idea for the novel Frankenstein while spending the summer with Lord Byron and John William Polidori near Geneva, Switzerland.
Married Percy Bysshe Shelley following the suicide of Percy's wife Harriet.
The Shelleys left Britain for Italy.
Published the novel Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus.
Her husband Percy Bysshe Shelley drowned when his sailing boat sank in a storm near Viareggio.
Returned to Britain.
Published the historical novel Valperga.
Published the apocalyptic novel The Last Man.
Wrote biographical articles for Dionysius Lardner's Cabinet Cyclopaedia.
Published the historical novel The Fortunes of Perkin Warbeck.
Published the novel Lodore.
Published the novel Falkner.
Published the travelogue Rambles in Germany and Italy.
Died of an illness suspected to be a brain tumor.

keyAchievements.title: Writing Frankenstein
keyAchievements.description: She wrote the Gothic novel Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus (1818), which is considered an early example of science fiction.

keyAchievements.title: Editing and Promoting Her Husband's Works
keyAchievements.description: She edited and promoted the works of her husband, the romantic poet and philosopher Percy Bysshe Shelley.

faq.question: What work is Mary Shelley best known for?
faq.answer: Until the 1970s, she was known primarily for her novel Frankenstein and her efforts to publish her husband's works.

faq.question: Who were Mary Shelley's parents?
faq.answer: Her father was the political philosopher William Godwin, and her mother was the philosopher and women's rights advocate Mary Wollstonecraft.

faq.question: What themes are mainly dealt with in Mary Shelley's works?
faq.answer: Her works often argue that cooperation and sympathy, particularly as practiced by women in the family, are the ways to reform civil society.

timeline.event: Born at York House near the Strand in London.
Entered Trinity College, Cambridge.
Started his legal career by enrolling at Gray's Inn.
Returned home after his father's death.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Bossiney in Cornwall.
Became an outer barrister.
Became a bencher, a senior member of the Inn.
Elected as a Reader, a law lecturer of the Inn.
Became a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Acted as a secret advisor to Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex.
Wrote Observations on a Libel, refuting the Jesuit Robert Parsons.
Elected as a Member of Parliament for Middlesex.
Became Queen's Counsel after being named legal advisor to Elizabeth I.
Arrested for failing to pay off debts.
Participated in the interrogation of the Earl of Essex for treason.
Received a knighthood as a Knight Bachelor.
Married Alice Barnham, daughter of a London alderman.
Published The Advancement of Learning.
Took the post of Solicitor General.
Officially served as a clerk of the Star Chamber.
Became Attorney General.
Prosecuted Robert Carr, Earl of Somerset, and his wife Frances Howard for murder, leading to a guilty verdict.
Sat as a regent for a month.
Appointed Lord Chancellor and became Baron Verulam.
Published Novum Organum.
Elevated to Viscount St Albans.
Impeached by Parliament on charges of bribery and stripped of all public offices and titles.
Died of pneumonia.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Modern Empiricism and Laying the Groundwork for the Scientific Revolution
keyAchievements.description: He researched natural philosophy and led the development of the scientific method, laying the groundwork for the later Scientific Revolution, and is evaluated alongside Descartes as a pioneer of modern philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Advocating Empiricist Philosophy and Writing Novum Organum
keyAchievements.description: He argued for empiricism, stating that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and wrote Novum Organum, which criticized Aristotle's methodology and advocated empiricism.

keyAchievements.title: Publishing The Advancement of Learning
keyAchievements.description: In The Advancement of Learning, he classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation for future pursuits of truth.

keyAchievements.title: Famous Maxim 'Knowledge is Power'
keyAchievements.description: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

keyAchievements.title: Proposing a Library Classification System
keyAchievements.description: He patronized libraries and proposed a classification system dividing books into three categories: history, poetry, and philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Act of Union
keyAchievements.description: Arguing for the annexation of Scotland, he was evaluated as a pioneer of the Act of Union that later laid the foundation for the creation of the Kingdom of Great Britain.

faq.question: How is Francis Bacon evaluated?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as the founder of modern empiricism and, alongside Descartes, a pioneer of modern philosophy, leading the development of the scientific method to lay the groundwork for the Scientific Revolution.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's representative philosophical claims?
faq.answer: He advocated empiricism, claiming that the nature of the natural world could be revealed through inductive reasoning, and proposed a method of arriving at truth through careful observation and experimentation by eliminating preconceptions.

faq.question: What are Francis Bacon's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include Novum Organum, which advocated empiricism, and The Advancement of Learning, which classified the system of knowledge and preached the importance of inductive observation.

faq.question: What famous quote did Francis Bacon leave?
faq.answer: He is well known for the phrase "Knowledge is power" (scientia est potentia).

faq.question: What public offices did Francis Bacon hold?
faq.answer: He served as Attorney General and Lord Chancellor of England during the reign of James I, and at that time, the Lord Chancellor doubled as the Prime Minister.

timeline.event: Born in Majae, Chobu-myeon, Gwangju-bu, Gyeonggi-do.
After his father Jeong Jae-won resigned from his government post and returned to his rural home, he named Jeong Yak-yong's childhood name Gwinong.
Married Pungsan Hong, the daughter of military official Hong Hwa-bo.
His father Jeong Jae-won became the assistant secretary of the Board of Taxation and rented a house in Seoul, where he met Lee Ga-hwan and Lee Seung-hun.
Participated in the Western Learning doctrine training session at Jueosa Temple and Cheonjinam Hermitage in Yangju, Gyeonggi-do, led by Gwon Cheol-sin.
Passed the Jeungwangsi examination celebrating the investiture of the Crown Prince and became a Saengwon (licentiate) through the Hoesi examination.
Heard an explanation of Catholic doctrines from Lee Byeok, the brother-in-law of his eldest brother Jeong Yak-hyeon.
The Myeongnyebang Incident occurred, where the Myeongnyebang community run by Lee Seung-hun was discovered and he was dragged to the Board of Punishments.
King Jeongjo defined Catholicism as an evil cult and issued a ban, and ordered the search and burning of Western Learning books related to Catholicism.
Passed the highest-level state examination at the age of 27 and entered government service.
When the Jinsan Incident occurred, where Yun Ji-chung held his mother's funeral in a Catholic manner in Jinsan, Jeolla-do, he was exiled to Haemi, Seosan, but was released after 11 days.
Participated in the design of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress and utilized the Geojunggi (traditional crane).
His father Jeong Jae-won passed away.
Lectured at Seonggyungwan and patrolled Yeoncheon, Saknyeong, etc. as a secret royal inspector of Gyeonggi-do.
Demoted to Geumjeong Chalbang (station master) due to the Eulmyo Persecution incident.
Due to suspicions raised by Busagwa Bak Jang-seol, Lee Seung-hun was demoted to Yesan, Lee Ga-hwan to Chungju Moksa, and Jeong Yak-yong to Hongju Geumjeong Chalbang.
While serving as the Third Minister of the Board of Punishments, he was impeached by his older brother Jeong Yak-jeon, so he submitted a "Jamyeongso" (petition of self-vindication) and resigned.
While living in retirement in his hometown, he heard the news of King Jeongjo's death and went up to Seoul.
Queen Dowager Jeongsun issued an order suppressing Catholicism and began a purge of the Namin faction.
Arrested and imprisoned, then his sentence was reduced to exile and he was released.
Began 18 years of exile in places like Janggi, Gyeongsang-do, and Gangjin, Jeolla-do.
His son Jeong Nong-jang (Nong-a) died of smallpox.
His exile was lifted and he rose to the position of Seungji (Royal Secretary).
Returned to his hometown.
While in exile in Gangjin-gun, Jeolla-do, he conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality.
Passed away at his home in Mahyeon-ri.
Posthumously promoted to Jeongheon Daebu Gyujanggak Jehak and granted the posthumous title 'Mundo'.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Writing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Known as the person who wrote the most books since the invention of Chinese characters, authoring over 500 volumes.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Mokminsimseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book that details the principles and mindset of local magistrates who govern the people.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Heumheumsinseo"
keyAchievements.description: A book detailing the principles and norms of judgments, punishments, and prison administration based on actual cases he investigated while serving as the magistrate of Goksan.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Gyeongseyupyo"
keyAchievements.description: A book discussing appropriate and standard institutional regulations for state management.

keyAchievements.title: Writing "Magwahoetong"
keyAchievements.description: Authored the measles treatment book "Magwahoetong" based on Lee Heon-gil's "Majingibang," saving the lives of countless Joseon people.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Geojunggi
keyAchievements.description: Created the pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) for use during the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of Silhak
keyAchievements.description: Synthesized the Silhak (Practical Learning) ideology handed down through Yu Hyeong-won and Seongho Yi Ik into a comprehensive system.

keyAchievements.title: Conception of Jeongjeon-ron
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the Jeongjeon-ron (well-field system) to improve land inequality through the free distribution of land and communal labor-communal distribution.

keyAchievements.title: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure
keyAchievements.description: Selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012 to commemorate the 250th anniversary of his birth.

faq.question: When was Jeong Yak-yong born and when did he die?
