faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was born on August 5, 1762, and passed away on April 7, 1836.

faq.question: What are the major works of Jeong Yak-yong?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong's three major works are "Mokminsimseo", "Heumheumsinseo", and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: What was Jeong Yak-yong's relationship with Catholicism?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong was once deeply engrossed in Catholicism but apostatized after the Sinhae Persecution (1791), and actively participated in searching for Catholic believers during the Sinyu Persecution (1801).

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong do during his exile?
faq.answer: During his 18-year exile, Jeong Yak-yong authored extensive writings, including "Mokminsimseo" and "Gyeongseyupyo".

faq.question: How did Jeong Yak-yong contribute to the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress?
faq.answer: During the construction of Suwon Hwaseong Fortress, Jeong Yak-yong created a pulley-and-windlass system for lifting heavy objects and invented the Geojunggi (traditional crane) to utilize it.

faq.question: What did Jeong Yak-yong's ideology aim for?
faq.answer: Jeong Yak-yong aimed to establish Silhak of statecraft and practical use, correct distorted theories by returning to the essence of Confucianism of Confucius and Mencius, and build a welfare state that cares for the socially and economically weak.

faq.question: Was Jeong Yak-yong selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure?
faq.answer: Yes, Jeong Yak-yong was selected as a UNESCO World Commemorative Figure in 2012, marking the 250th anniversary of his birth.

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

timeline.event: Period of living as the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha
Possibility of birth
Possibility of accession to the throne
Conquest of the Kalinga region
Record of visiting Sambodhi (the sacred Bodhi tree of Bodh Gaya)
Became closer to the Buddhist Sangha and toured the empire
Began inscribing edicts to spread Dharma
Established the position of Dhamma-Mahamatra
Commissioned the expansion of the Stupa of Buddha Kanakamuni
Donated the Khalatika Cave

keyAchievements.title: Ruling the Empire
keyAchievements.description: As the 3rd Mauryan Emperor of Magadha, he ruled an empire that covered the largest part of the Indian subcontinent.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage and Spread of Buddhism
keyAchievements.description: As a patron of Buddhism, he played a crucial role in spreading Buddhism throughout ancient Asia.

keyAchievements.title: Spread of Dhamma
keyAchievements.description: After the conquest of Kalinga, he dedicated himself to the spread of 'Dhamma', or righteous behavior.

keyAchievements.title: Construction and Support of Buddhist Facilities
keyAchievements.description: Buddhist legends praise him for building a large number of stupas, patronizing the Third Buddhist Council, supporting Buddhist missionaries, and making generous donations to the Sangha.

keyAchievements.title: Historical Stature in India
keyAchievements.description: Since the decipherment of the Brahmi script in the 19th century, he has been renowned as one of India's greatest emperors.

keyAchievements.title: Symbol of Modern India
keyAchievements.description: The emblem of the Republic of India is an adaptation of the Lion Capital of Ashoka, and the Ashoka Chakra, the wheel of Ashoka, is depicted in the center of the Indian flag.

keyAchievements.title: Social Welfare Activities
keyAchievements.description: Carried out social welfare activities such as establishing medical facilities for humans and animals, cultivating medicinal herbs, digging wells, and planting trees along roads.

keyAchievements.title: Promotion of Animal Welfare
keyAchievements.description: Promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, and abolishing royal hunts.

faq.question: What does Ashoka's name mean?
faq.answer: 'A-shoka' literally means 'without sorrow'.

faq.question: What were Ashoka's main titles?
faq.answer: His main titles were 'Priyadasi' ('he who regards amiably' or 'of gracious mien') and 'Devanampiya' ('beloved of the gods').

faq.question: Was Ashoka a Buddhist?
faq.answer: In his Minor Rock Edict 1, he clearly identified himself as a Buddhist by calling himself an upasaka (Buddhist lay follower) and a Shakya (Buddhist).

faq.question: Did Ashoka persecute other religions?
faq.answer: His edicts emphasize tolerance for all sects, donating caves to non-Buddhist ascetics and stating that both Brahmins and Shramanas deserve respect.

faq.question: What did 'Dhamma' mean to Ashoka?
faq.answer: To Ashoka, Dhamma meant a moral policy encompassing active social concern, religious tolerance, ecological awareness, observance of common ethical precepts, and the renunciation of war.

faq.question: Was Ashoka a violent person before his conversion to Buddhism?
faq.answer: Both the Sri Lankan and North Indian traditions claim that Ashoka was a violent person before embracing Buddhism.

faq.question: How did the Kalinga War affect Ashoka?
faq.answer: The destruction that occurred during the war evoked remorse for violence in him, and in the years that followed, he was drawn to Buddhism.

faq.question: What efforts did Ashoka make for animal welfare?
faq.answer: He promoted animal welfare by abolishing the death penalty, banning the slaughter of animals for sacrifice, abolishing royal hunts, and establishing animal hospitals.

timeline.event: Born in Oak Park, Illinois.
Attended Oak Park and River Forest High School.
Published his first article in the school newspaper, 'The Trapeze'.
Severely wounded by mortar fire while serving as an ambulance driver for the Italian Red Cross, and received the Silver Medal of Military Valor.
Returned to the United States and began working as a freelancer and staff writer for the 'Toronto Star Weekly'.
Married Hadley Richardson, moved to Paris, and worked as a foreign correspondent for the 'Toronto Star'.
Met Ezra Pound at Sylvia Beach's bookstore, Shakespeare and Company.
Published his first book 'Three Stories and Ten Poems' and his son Jack Hemingway was born.
Published 'In Our Time' and met F. Scott Fitzgerald.
Published his first novel 'The Sun Also Rises'.
Divorced Hadley Richardson, married Pauline Pfeiffer, and published the short story collection 'Men Without Women'.
His son Patrick was born, and he received news of his father's suicide.
Published the novel 'A Farewell to Arms'.
Broke his right arm in a car accident.
His third son, Gregory Hemingway, was born.
Went on a safari trip to Kenya with Pauline.
Purchased the fishing boat Pilar.
Published 'Green Hills of Africa'.
Traveled to Spain to cover the Spanish Civil War for the North American Newspaper Alliance, and published 'To Have and Have Not'.
Began living at the Hotel Ambos Mundos in Havana, Cuba.
Married Martha Gellhorn and published the novel 'For Whom the Bell Tolls'.
Traveled to China with Martha.
Stayed in London and other parts of Europe, and participated as a journalist in the Normandy landings and the liberation of Paris.
Divorced Martha Gellhorn.
Married Mary Welsh.
Awarded the Bronze Star Medal for his bravery during World War II.
Published the novel 'Across the River and into the Trees'.
Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' and embarked on a second trip to Africa.
Survived two plane crashes in Africa and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Left Cuba and purchased a house on the outskirts of Ketchum, Idaho.
Left Cuba for New York and was admitted to the Mayo Clinic.
Returned to Ketchum after being discharged from the Mayo Clinic, and committed suicide with a shotgun on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: Stylistic innovation
keyAchievements.description: Profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction with a concise, understated writing style known as the Iceberg Theory.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Literature
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

keyAchievements.title: Pulitzer Prize
keyAchievements.description: Won the Pulitzer Prize for 'The Old Man and the Sea' in 1952.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of major works
keyAchievements.description: Published numerous masterpieces including 'The Sun Also Rises' (1926), 'A Farewell to Arms' (1929), 'For Whom the Bell Tolls' (1940), and 'The Old Man and the Sea' (1951).

keyAchievements.title: Bronze Star Medal
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Bronze Star Medal in 1947 for his bravery during World War II.

keyAchievements.title: Literary influence
keyAchievements.description: Greatly influenced the overall style of American literature and was inducted into the Chicago Literary Hall of Fame in 2012.

faq.question: What is Ernest Hemingway's signature writing style?
faq.answer: Hemingway profoundly influenced 20th-century fiction by using a concise, understated writing style known as the 'Iceberg Theory'.

faq.question: When did Hemingway win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
faq.answer: Hemingway won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.

faq.question: What are some of Hemingway's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'The Sun Also Rises', 'A Farewell to Arms', 'For Whom the Bell Tolls', and 'The Old Man and the Sea'.

faq.question: How did Hemingway's life end?
faq.answer: On July 2, 1961, he committed suicide with a shotgun in Ketchum, Idaho.

faq.question: What are the main themes of Hemingway's works?
faq.answer: Love, war, the wilderness, and defeat frequently appear as main themes in his works, with nature often depicted as a place of rebirth and rest.

timeline.event: Born in Clermont-Ferrand, France, to Etienne Pascal, a tax official in Rouen
Discovered by himself that the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180 degrees
Discovered Pascal's triangle
Participated in the main regular meetings of a group of French mathematicians
Proved Pascal's theorem, which is the foundation of projective geometry
Derived 400 propositions using Pascal's theorem
Invented the Pascaline, the first calculator
Established Pascal's law, which laid the foundation for hydrostatics, through an experiment using a mercury column
Conducted a vacuum experiment using various shaped tubes in Rouen
His experiments were conducted in Paris by Mersenne and other scientists
Published 'Discours sur le vide' (Thoughts on the Vacuum)
Moved from Rouen to Paris due to failing health
Debated with Descartes on the existence of a vacuum
Wrote a paper titled 'Experiences nouvelles touchant le vide' (New Experiments with the Vacuum)
Performed 'Pascal's experiment' observing the change in the height of a mercury column with altitude at Puy de Dome
Wrote a paper summarizing the results of the Puy de Dome experiment
Suspended his mathematics and science research to focus his mind on religious contemplation
Explained the law of pressure (Pascal's principle) in 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' (Treatises on the Equilibrium of Liquids)
Involved in a carriage accident
Wrote a confession of faith claiming to have heard a voice from heaven and became deeply involved in Christian theology
Suffered mentally while battling headaches
Gave his house to a poor family suffering from smallpox and moved into his sister's house
Died from convulsions
After his death, 'Traitez de l'equilibre des liqueurs' was published by Perier

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's triangle
keyAchievements.description: A geometric arrangement of binomial coefficients in the shape of a triangle in mathematics.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's theorem
keyAchievements.description: A theorem that is the foundation of projective geometry, which he proved at the age of 16.

keyAchievements.title: Pascaline (Pascal's calculator)
keyAchievements.description: The world's first calculator, invented at the age of 19, capable of addition and subtraction.

keyAchievements.title: Pascal's principle
keyAchievements.description: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for probability theory
keyAchievements.description: Co-founded the foundation of probability theory through correspondence with Fermat.

keyAchievements.title: Cycloid research
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the development of mathematics by studying the cycloid to forget his headaches.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Pensees'
keyAchievements.description: An unfinished book containing his Christian apologetics, including the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'Provincial Letters'
keyAchievements.description: A book written to defend Jansenist clergy oppressed by the Jesuits, evaluated as the starting point of modern French prose.

faq.question: Who was Blaise Pascal?
faq.answer: A French psychologist, mathematician, scientist, theologian, physicist, inventor, writer, philosopher, and statistician who invested a lot of time especially in philosophy and theology.

faq.question: What is the Pascal calculator?
faq.answer: The world's first calculator invented at the age of 19 to help his accountant father, capable only of addition and subtraction.

faq.question: What is Pascal's principle?
faq.answer: A law stating that if pressure is applied to a portion of a confined, incompressible fluid, that pressure is transmitted equally to all parts of the fluid.

faq.question: What is Pascal's famous saying?
faq.answer: He left the famous saying, 'Man is a thinking reed.'

faq.question: Why did Pascal stop his mathematics and science research?
faq.answer: At the age of 27, he focused his mind on religious contemplation and thus suspended his mathematics and science research.

timeline.event: Born on June 23
Started majoring in mathematics at the University of Cambridge
Took physicist Eddington's scientific methodology class and proved the central limit theorem
Left for Princeton University after providing a negative proof to the decidability problem
Finished studies at Princeton University and returned to Cambridge
Joined the Government Code and Cypher School (GCCS) during World War II and started cryptography research
Devised the Turing machine; pushed for the creation of a mathematics department to build an electronic calculator at the National Physical Laboratory (NPL) on October 1
Awarded the Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE)
Published a paper titled 'Can Machines Think?' in the philosophy journal 'Mind'
Arrested and convicted by British police on charges of homosexuality
Published a paper on the Riemann zeta function
Died of potassium cyanide poisoning on June 7
Queen Elizabeth II pardoned Turing's homosexuality conviction on December 24

keyAchievements.title: Devised the Turing machine
keyAchievements.description: Formalized the concepts of algorithm and computation through an abstract model called the Turing machine, making a profound contribution to the development of computer science.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Turing test
keyAchievements.description: Proposed an experiment to evaluate how human-like a machine's answers are with the proposition 'Can machines think?', achieving great success.

keyAchievements.title: Cracked the Enigma code
keyAchievements.description: Successfully cracked the German codes during World War II by introducing a code-breaking machine called the Bombe.

keyAchievements.title: Proved the central limit theorem
keyAchievements.description: Proved it in 1933 without knowing that Finnish mathematician Jarl Waldemar Lindeberg had already proved it in 1922.

keyAchievements.title: Negative proof of the decidability problem
keyAchievements.description: Provided a negative proof to the decidability problem in 1936.

faq.question: What fields was Alan Turing a pioneer in?
faq.answer: He was an English computer scientist, mathematician, cryptanalyst, logician, and a pioneering figure in computer science.

faq.question: What is the purpose of the Turing Award?
faq.answer: It is an annual prize given by the ACM to those who have made significant contributions to computer science, established in 1966 to honor Turing's contributions.

faq.question: Why is Alan Turing called the 'father of computer science'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'father of computer science' because he made profound contributions to theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence.

faq.question: When was Alan Turing pardoned for his homosexuality conviction?
faq.answer: Queen Elizabeth II pardoned his homosexuality conviction on December 24, 2013, 59 years after his death.

timeline.event: Born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid
Followed an Italian cardinal to Rome, became a soldier, and participated in the Battle of Lepanto, where he suffered an injury that left his left hand crippled
Captured by pirates and lived as a slave in Algiers for 5 years
Paid his ransom with the help of the Trinitarian Order, returned to Madrid, and began to write
Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not gain popularity
Published Part 1 of 'Don Quixote', which gained immense popularity
Completed Part 2 of 'Don Quixote'
Passed away at the age of 69

keyAchievements.title: Don Quixote
keyAchievements.description: A work evaluated by many as the first modern novel in Europe.

keyAchievements.title: La Galatea
keyAchievements.description: Published the novel 'La Galatea', but it did not attract much popularity.

faq.question: What is Miguel de Cervantes's nickname?
faq.answer: He has the nickname 'Prince of Wits' (el Príncipe de los Ingenios).

faq.question: Where was Miguel de Cervantes born?
faq.answer: He was born in Alcala de Henares, a university town in Madrid.

faq.question: What were Miguel de Cervantes's main professions?
faq.answer: He was a novelist, poet, and playwright.

faq.question: How are Miguel de Cervantes's works evaluated?
faq.answer: His works are mentioned among the most important masterpieces in all of literature, and 'Don Quixote' in particular is evaluated as the first modern novel in Europe.

timeline.event: Born
Created the masterpieces 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana'
Created the masterpiece 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' (Old pond / a frog jumps in / sound of water)
Died (November 28, lunar October 12)

keyAchievements.title: Recognition of Haikai-no-renga style
keyAchievements.description: His works were recognized during his lifetime in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Haiku
keyAchievements.description: Today, he is considered a master of haiku, which was called hokku during his time.

keyAchievements.title: Internationally renowned poet
keyAchievements.description: His poetry is internationally renowned, and many of his poems in Japan are reproduced at monuments or historical sites.

keyAchievements.title: Fame across Japan
keyAchievements.description: He entered the world of poetry at a young age, and by integrating himself into the intellectual landscape of Edo, he became famous throughout Japan.

keyAchievements.title: Inspiration through travel
keyAchievements.description: He wandered throughout Japan, including the north, east, and west, to gain inspiration for his works.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry based on experiences of nature
keyAchievements.description: His poetry was influenced by his direct experiences of the world around him, condensing emotions about landscapes into simple elements.

faq.question: Who was Matsuo Basho?
faq.answer: Matsuo Basho, born in 1644 and died on November 28, 1694 (lunar October 12), was a poet of the early Edo period. His childhood name was Kinsaku, and he was also known as Matsuo Chuemon Munefusa.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's major achievements?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, his works were recognized in the collaborative haikai-no-renga style, and today he is considered a master of haiku. His poetry is internationally renowned and reproduced at monuments and historical sites across Japan.

faq.question: What are Matsuo Basho's representative works?
faq.answer: Representative works include 'Inochi futatsu no naka ni ikitaru sakura kana' and 'Kumo oriori hito o yasumuru tsukimi kana' from 1685, and 'Furuike ya kawazu tobikomu mizu no oto' from 1686.

faq.question: How did Matsuo Basho conduct his literary activities?
faq.answer: While making a living as a teacher, he distanced himself from the social and urban life where literary circles were based, wandering throughout Japan to gain inspiration. His poetry was influenced by direct experiences of the surrounding world, condensing his emotions about landscapes.

faq.question: Which of his works did Matsuo Basho consider most important?
faq.answer: He believed his best work lay in participating in and leading renku, and considered the connection with haikai verses more important than hokku.

timeline.event: Born as the second child of Edward John Wollstonecraft and Elizabeth Dickson in Spitalfields, London
Became independent and worked as a companion to Sarah Dawson in Bath, Somerset
Returned home to care for her mother on her deathbed
Persuaded her sister Eliza, who was suffering from postpartum depression, to emancipate herself from her household
Left her school and followed her friend Fanny Blood to Lisbon to nurse her
Published 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters'; decided to become an author
Published her first novel 'Mary: A Fiction'; published her only children's book 'Original Stories from Real Life'
Wrote 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' in response to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France'
Wrote her most famous and influential work 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'; left for Paris in December and returned a month before Louis XVI's execution
France declared war on Britain
The Jacobin-led Committee of Public Safety seized power in France
Fell in love with American adventurer Gilbert Imlay; Imlay falsely registered Wollstonecraft as his wife to obtain American citizenship
Gave birth to her first daughter, Fanny Imlay
The Jacobins fell from power
'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution', written in Le Havre, Northern France, was published in London
Left France
Returned to London looking for Imlay, but Imlay refused to reconcile
Attempted suicide with laudanum but was saved by Imlay
Letters written to Imlay during her Scandinavian trip were compiled and published as 'Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark'
Moved to Somers Town, northwest London, with William Godwin
Gave birth to her second daughter, Mary
Died of puerperal fever (at age 38)
Her unfinished work 'Maria' was published posthumously
Her husband William Godwin published 'Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman'

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman' (1792)
keyAchievements.description: The first feminist philosophical work arguing that women are not naturally inferior to men, but appear to be only because they lack education, advocating for women's right to education and equal fundamental rights.

keyAchievements.title: 'A Vindication of the Rights of Men' (1790)
keyAchievements.description: A rebuttal to Edmund Burke's 'Reflections on the Revolution in France', and the first political and feminist writing to attack aristocracy, defend republicanism, and criticize Burke's gender-biased language.

keyAchievements.title: 'An Historical and Moral View of the French Revolution' (1794)
keyAchievements.description: A book that outlined the early history of the French Revolution, countered the counter-revolutionary atmosphere in Britain, and argued that the revolution was the result of social, economic, and political crises.

keyAchievements.title: 'Letters Written in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark' (1796)
keyAchievements.description: A travelogue containing reflections on the society and people of Scandinavia, questions of identity, and her relationship with Imlay, exploring the value of subjective experience and imagination, and advocating for women's liberation and education.

keyAchievements.title: Educational writings
keyAchievements.description: Argued through 'Thoughts on the Education of Daughters' (1787) and children's literature 'Original Stories from Real Life' (1788) that children should be taught to think and receive an education that fosters middle-class ethos.

faq.question: What is Mary Wollstonecraft's most famous book?
faq.answer: Her most famous book is 'A Vindication of the Rights of Woman', published in 1792.

faq.question: What did Mary Wollstonecraft argue about women's education?
faq.answer: She argued that women are not inherently inferior to men, but only appear to be due to a lack of education, and therefore should receive quality education.

faq.question: Are any of Mary Wollstonecraft's daughters famous?
faq.answer: Yes, her second daughter, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, later married Percy Bysshe Shelley and became Mary Shelley, the author of the novel 'Frankenstein'.

faq.question: With which philosophers did Mary Wollstonecraft associate?
faq.answer: She associated with radical writer Thomas Paine, philosopher William Godwin, and others, and notably married William Godwin.

faq.question: What was Mary Wollstonecraft's stance on the French Revolution?
faq.answer: She condemned the Jacobin regime and the Reign of Terror, but maintained the position that the revolution was a great achievement.

timeline.event: Born to Pepin the Short and Bertrada of Laon in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.
His father, Pepin the Short, became King of the Franks with the approval of Pope Zachary.
Pope Stephen II bestowed the title of King on Pepin and his sons Charles and Carloman.
Succeeded to the throne after the death of his father Pepin the Short, taking over Austrasia and the northern regions.
Became sole ruler after the death of his brother Carloman I.
Began attacking the Saxons.
Destroyed and annexed the Lombard Kingdom in northern Italy.
Attended the Easter festival in Rome with the Pope and reaffirmed the promise to transfer the Papal States.
Suffered a crushing defeat by the Basques at the Battle of Roncevaux Pass during an expedition to the Iberian Peninsula.
Visited Rome, had his two sons Pepin and Louis consecrated, and bestowed upon them the crowns of Lombardy and Aquitaine respectively.
His wife Hildegard and mother Bertrada of Laon passed away successively, and he remarried Fastrada.
Conquered Thuringia and Bavaria, and deposed Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria.
Soundly defeated the Avars along the Danube River and drove them beyond the borders.
Built a palace and royal church in Aachen, northwestern Germany, making it the de facto capital.
Pope Leo III, attacked by opponents, fled to Charlemagne's court to seek assistance.
Was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III in St. Peter's Basilica.
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
The Avar Khagan Zodan visited Aachen, swore fealty to Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chiefs, including Viseslav, voluntarily surrendered.
Eastern Roman Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son Louis I the Pious as the legitimate successor at the court in Aachen and crowned him co-emperor.
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Pope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne as a saint at the request of Frederick I.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
keyAchievements.description: Greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by conquering most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Crowned Emperor of the Romans
keyAchievements.description: Received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800, politically and religiously unifying most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Carolingian Renaissance
keyAchievements.description: Initiated the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing arts, religion, and culture through the church during his reign.

keyAchievements.title: Codification of laws and centralization
keyAchievements.description: Codified the laws of various tribes to rule over vast territories and enabled centralized rule by dispatching officials from the center.

keyAchievements.title: Spread and integration of Christianity
keyAchievements.description: Sought ideological unity by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of cultural and educational institutions
keyAchievements.description: Led a revival of learning and arts by building a palace and royal church in Aachen, and founding a royal library and palace school.

faq.question: When was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: There are theories that Charlemagne was born on April 2 of 740 or 742, or in 747.

faq.question: Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
faq.answer: He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the foundation for the current identity of Europe.

faq.question: When did Charlemagne become Emperor of the Romans?
faq.answer: Charlemagne received the title of Emperor of the Romans from Pope Leo III in December 800.

faq.question: How tall was Charlemagne?
faq.answer: According to Einhard's records and the results of a tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne was about 190 to 191 cm tall.

faq.question: What languages did Charlemagne speak?
faq.answer: In addition to his mother tongue, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak some Greek.

faq.question: Where was Charlemagne born?
faq.answer: Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, Frankish Kingdom, near Liege, Belgium.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

Odaenathus was assassinated, and Zenobia became the regent of the Palmyrene Empire in place of her young son Vaballathus
Zenobia successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt
Declared Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and herself as Empress of Palmyra
Defeated by Roman Emperor Aurelian and taken to Rome as a captive, bringing the Palmyrene Empire to ruin

로마 제국의 아우렐리아누스 황제가 팔미라 제국 정복을 결심하고 군대를 이끌고 침공함.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of the Palmyrene Empire
keyAchievements.description: After her husband's death, she ruled the Palmyrene Empire as regent and expanded its territory by conquering Roman Egypt.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of an independent state
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to establish an independent state from the Roman Empire by capturing large areas of Roman territory, including the Levant and Asia Minor.

keyAchievements.title: Declaration of Imperial titles
keyAchievements.description: Declared her son Vaballathus as emperor of the Roman Empire and elevated herself to Empress of Palmyra.

faq.question: Who was Zenobia?
faq.answer: A noblewoman and ruler of the Palmyrene Empire, who acted as regent after her husband Odaenathus was assassinated, bringing the empire to its peak before clashing with the Roman Empire and eventually leading to its downfall.

faq.question: What territories did Zenobia conquer?
faq.answer: She successfully commanded the army to conquer Roman Egypt, the Levant, and Asia Minor.

faq.question: How did Zenobia's rule come to an end?
faq.answer: She was defeated by the newly ascended Roman Emperor Aurelian, taken to Rome as a captive, and the Palmyrene Empire fell.

faq.question: 제노비아는 어떻게 팔미라 제국의 통치자가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 267년 오데나투스 황제 암살 후 아들 바발라투스를 후계자로 임명하며 팔미라 제국의 공동 통치자가 되었습니다.

faq.question: 제노비아의 최후는 어떻게 되었나요?
faq.answer: 274년 로마에서 열린 아우렐리아누스 황제의 개선식에 포로로 끌려왔으나, 그 이후의 정확한 생사는 불명확합니다.

timeline.event: Born to a Protestant officer father in Montbeliard, Württemberg
Enrolled in the Caroline Academy (Karlsschule) in Stuttgart and spent 4 years

keyAchievements.title: Founded comparative anatomy
keyAchievements.description: Earned the title of the 'Father of Comparative Anatomy' for establishing the foundation of comparative anatomy by systematically comparing the anatomical structures of various animals.

keyAchievements.title: Founded vertebrate paleontology
keyAchievements.description: Established vertebrate paleontology by studying fossil bones and proposing the theory of extinction.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the principle of correlation of parts
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'principle of correlation of parts', stating that the shape and function of each organ in an organism are closely linked to the overall structure and lifestyle of the animal.

faq.question: What is Georges Cuvier's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He is the French zoologist known for founding comparative anatomy and paleontology.

faq.question: What is catastrophism advocated by Cuvier?
faq.answer: It is the theory that sudden, widespread catastrophic events like massive floods occurred multiple times in Earth's past history, causing the extinction of species.

faq.question: What was Cuvier's view on evolution?
faq.answer: He opposed the theory of evolution. According to his principle of correlation of parts, an organism's structure is perfectly adapted to its lifestyle, so he believed that changes in some parts would break the overall harmony, making survival impossible.

faq.question: How did Cuvier prove that extinction occurred?
faq.answer: He proved that extinction was a real phenomenon by demonstrating that fossil elephant bones, like mammoths and mastodons, belonged to different species than living elephants.

faq.question: 조르주 퀴비에는 파리 박물관에서 어떻게 교수가 되었나요?
faq.answer: 그는 생틸르의 소개로 파리 박물관의 교수가 되었습니다.

The Pope excommunicates Elizabeth
Francis Drake completes his circumnavigation of the globe
Executes Mary, Queen of Scots
The English Navy defeats the Spanish Armada
Gives the 'Golden Speech' to Parliament
Passes away at Richmond Palace

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Church of England
keyAchievements.description: Through the Elizabethan Religious Settlement, she firmly established the Church of England as the state religion and sought stability amidst religious conflict.

keyAchievements.title: Defeat of the Spanish Armada
keyAchievements.description: Defeated the Invincible Armada of Spain in 1588, achieving maritime supremacy.

keyAchievements.title: Flourishing of culture and arts
keyAchievements.description: Great playwrights like William Shakespeare and Christopher Marlowe were active during her reign, marking a golden age of English literature.

keyAchievements.title: Support for overseas expansion
keyAchievements.description: Supported overseas exploration, such as Francis Drake's circumnavigation, and laid the foundation for the colonial empire, including establishing the East India Company.

keyAchievements.title: National integration and stability
keyAchievements.description: Maintained domestic political stability and national integration over a 44-year reign, ushering in the 'Elizabethan era'.

keyAchievements.title: 스페인 무적함대 격파
keyAchievements.description: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 무적함대가 잉글랜드 해군의 공격에 대패하는 데 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가
keyAchievements.description: 1600년 12월 31일 잉글랜드 동인도 회사를 인가하여 해외 식민지 개척의 기반을 마련했습니다.

faq.question: Did Elizabeth I ever marry?
faq.answer: She never married, earning the nickname 'The Virgin Queen'. She utilized her marriage prospects as a diplomatic tool but ultimately famously stated she was married to the nation.

faq.question: Why did she execute Mary, Queen of Scots?
faq.answer: Mary, Queen of Scots, was a Catholic and the next in line to the English throne, becoming a focal point for rebellions and assassination plots against Elizabeth, which led to her eventual execution.

faq.question: What is the relationship between Elizabeth I and the English Renaissance?
faq.answer: Her reign is regarded as the golden age of the English Renaissance, flourishing with culture, arts, and overseas exploration, often called the 'Elizabethan era'.

faq.question: Who succeeded Elizabeth I?
faq.answer: Since she left no heirs, James VI of Scotland (son of Mary, Queen of Scots) succeeded her as James I of England, bringing the Tudor dynasty to an end and starting the Stuart dynasty.

faq.question: 엘리자베스 1세 재위 중 가장 중요한 대외 사건은 무엇인가요?
faq.answer: 스페인과의 잉글랜드-스페인 전쟁에서 스페인의 무적함대를 격파한 사건이 가장 중요하며, 해외 식민지 개척과 잉글랜드 동인도 회사 인가도 이루어졌습니다.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Participated as the third officer in the British National Antarctic Expedition led by Robert Falcon Scott from 1901 to 1903.

keyAchievements.title: Nimrod Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition from 1907 to 1909, achieving the record of Farthest South latitude at 88° 23' S.

keyAchievements.title: Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Although the attempt to cross Antarctica between 1914 and 1917 failed due to the sinking of the Endurance, he demonstrated miraculous leadership by safely rescuing all 27 crew members.

keyAchievements.title: Shackleton-Rowett Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Undertook his final Antarctic expedition from 1921 to 1922 but died during the journey.

keyAchievements.title: 남극점 육로 발견
keyAchievements.description: 1902년 남극대륙의 육로 여행으로 남극점까지 이어진 길을 최초로 발견했다.

faq.question: What is Shackleton's greatest achievement?
faq.answer: During the Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition, when his ship was crushed by ice and sank, he managed to lead all his crew members through extreme environments for about two years and returned safely.

faq.question: Why did the Endurance sink?
faq.answer: In January 1915, the Endurance became trapped in the Weddell Sea ice pack in Antarctica, and was eventually crushed by the pressure of the ice and sank in November of the same year.

faq.question: How did Shackleton rescue his crew?
faq.answer: He crossed perilous seas on the 22-foot lifeboat James Caird to reach South Georgia Island, crossed treacherous mountains with two others to reach a whaling station, and eventually organized a rescue ship.

faq.question: What is the status of the Endurance shipwreck today?
faq.answer: In March 2022, 107 years after its sinking, the wreck of the Endurance was discovered at a depth of 3,008 meters in the Weddell Sea in an excellently preserved condition.

faq.question: 어니스트 섀클턴은 어떤 훈장을 받았나요?
faq.answer: 1907년 로열 빅토리아 훈장 4등급, 1909년 로열 빅토리아 훈장 3등급과 기사작위, 1919년 군(軍)부문 대영 제국 훈장 4등급을 받았습니다.

timeline.event: Born as the twelfth child of Franz Theodor Schubert and Elisabeth in Himmelpfortgrund, Vienna.
Father bought a school in Lichtental and moved.
Received piano instruction from his older brother Ignaz and violin instruction from his father.
Michael Holzer, the organist and choir director of the parish church in Lichtental, taught Schubert singing, organ, and basso continuo.
Studied composition with Antonio Salieri.
Auditioned successfully to become a choirboy at the Imperial Court Chapel and entered the Stadtkonvikt.
Voice changed and left the Imperial Court Chapel choir.
Returned to his father's school.
Began teacher training at a normal school in Vienna.
Started working as an assistant teacher at his father's school in Lichtental.
Composed his first masterpiece, 'Gretchen am Spinnrade' (Gretchen at the Spinning Wheel).
Mother passed away.
Composed many Lieder, including 'Erlkönig' (Erlking) and 'Heidenröslein' (Heath Rose).
Left teaching and became an independent composer.
Worked as a music tutor for the daughters of Count Johann Karl Esterhazy in Zseliz, Hungary.
Signs of a serious illness (presumed to be syphilis) began to appear.
The first 'Schubertiade', a gathering to appreciate Schubert's music, was held at the home of Ignaz von Sonnleithner.
Deteriorating health.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of German Lieder
keyAchievements.description: Composed over 600 art songs (Lieder), elevating the Lied, previously considered a minor genre, to an important art form where music and poetry are equal.

keyAchievements.title: Expansion of Harmony and Melody
keyAchievements.description: Exhibited outstanding melodic genius and used rich harmonies with bold modulations, pointing the way toward the Romantic era.

keyAchievements.title: Symphonies
keyAchievements.description: The Symphony No. 8 'Unfinished' and Symphony No. 9 'The Great' are highly acclaimed for maximizing the possibilities of early Romantic symphonies.

keyAchievements.title: Piano and Chamber Music
keyAchievements.description: Left behind masterpieces that combine lyricism and dramatic tension, such as the late piano sonatas, 'Wanderer Fantasy', 'Trout Quintet', and String Quartet 'Death and the Maiden'.

keyAchievements.title: 피아노 5중주곡 《송어》
keyAchievements.description: 현악 4중주에 제2바이올린 대신 콘트라베이스를 사용한 독특한 구성의 피아노 5중주곡 《송어》를 작곡했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 가곡 《마왕》
keyAchievements.description: 17세에 괴테의 시를 바탕으로 한 가곡 《마왕》을 작곡했습니다.

faq.question: What is Schubert's nickname?
faq.answer: He is called the 'King of Lieder'.

faq.question: Did Schubert get married?
faq.answer: He never married and left no children.

faq.question: Did Schubert receive regular musical education?
faq.answer: He learned singing, piano, and violin from an early age and studied composition under Antonio Salieri, receiving education from the Imperial Court Chapel.

faq.question: Was Schubert rich?
faq.answer: Since he did not have a regular job or aristocratic patronage, he suffered from financial difficulties throughout his life and received much help from his friends.

faq.question: How did Schubert die?
faq.answer: The exact cause of death is unknown, but it is generally thought that his health deteriorated due to long-standing syphilis, and he ultimately died of typhoid fever.

timeline.event: Born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi
Emancipation Proclamation issued, freeing her family
Parents and younger brother die of yellow fever; Ida takes responsibility for her siblings
Forcibly removed from a first-class train car; files a lawsuit against the railroad company
Wins the lawsuit at the local court
The Tennessee Supreme Court overturns the ruling in favor of the railroad company
A friend's lynching incident occurs; she begins a full-scale anti-lynching campaign in 'The Free Speech and Headlight'
Her newspaper office is destroyed by a white mob; she relocates to Chicago
Publishes the anti-lynching pamphlet 'Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases'
Publishes another anti-lynching pamphlet 'The Red Record'
Marries Ferdinand L. Barnett
Participates in the founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP)

keyAchievements.title: Anti-lynching Journalism
keyAchievements.description: Through investigative journalism, she systematically investigated and exposed the reality that the lynching of Black people in the South was often used to eliminate Black people who achieved economic success, rather than to punish criminals.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Anti-lynching Pamphlets
keyAchievements.description: Published pamphlets containing the realities of lynching, such as 'Southern Horrors' (1892) and 'The Red Record' (1895), drawing national and international attention to the issue.

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Civil Rights Organizations
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the founding of the National Association of Colored Women (NACW) in 1896 and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in 1909.

faq.question: What kind of environment was Ida B. Wells born into?
faq.answer: She was born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi in 1862, but was freed the following year by the Emancipation Proclamation.

faq.question: What motivated Ida B. Wells to start her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: In 1892, a close friend and two other Black men were lynched by a white mob out of economic jealousy over their successful grocery store. This incident prompted her to begin a full-scale anti-lynching campaign.

faq.question: What challenges did Ida B. Wells face because of her anti-lynching campaign?
faq.answer: Her newspaper office in Memphis was destroyed by a white mob, and she received numerous death threats, eventually forcing her to leave the South and relocate to Chicago.

faq.question: What is the Alpha Suffrage Club?
faq.answer: It is the first Black women's suffrage organization in Illinois, founded by Ida B. Wells in Chicago in 1913.

timeline.event: Born in Marton, Yorkshire, England
Began working as an apprentice on a merchant ship
Volunteered for the Royal Navy
Promoted to ship's master; participated in the Seven Years' War and surveyed the St. Lawrence River in Canada
Married Elizabeth Batts
Commanded the HMS Endeavour on his first voyage, tasked with observing the transit of Venus and searching for the 'Unknown Southern Land' (Terra Australis)
Arrived in Tahiti and observed the transit of Venus
Explored the coastline of New Zealand and discovered the Cook Strait
Arrived on the eastern coast of Australia (Botany Bay) and claimed it for Britain
The Endeavour was damaged on the Great Barrier Reef, undergoing repairs
Returned to England, completing the first voyage
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his second voyage, circumnavigating the globe as far south as possible to search for the 'Unknown Southern Land'
Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time in history
Returned to England, completing the second voyage; proved that a vast habitable southern continent did not exist
Commanded the HMS Resolution on his third voyage to discover the Northwest Passage
Discovered the Hawaiian Islands and named them the 'Sandwich Islands'
Explored the western coast of North America and reached the Bering Strait but was blocked by ice
Returned to Hawaii, got into a conflict with the indigenous people, and was killed
힉스곶으로 명명했던 육지를 목격하며 오스트레일리아 대륙 동쪽 해안에 도달한 최초의 유럽인이 됨
오스트레일리아 대륙에 처음 상륙 (현재 커널반도)
그레이트 배리어 리프 북쪽에 인데버 호가 부딪혀 난파
포제션섬에 상륙하여 오스트레일리아 동해안의 영국 영유권을 선언
바타비아에서 말라리아와 이질로 많은 일행이 사망한 후 귀국
인데버호가 남 잉글랜드의 다운즈에 닻을 내리고 쿡은 켄트로 하선
유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입 (두 번째 항해)
통가, 이스터섬, 뉴칼레도니아, 바누아투에 도착 후 남아메리카 남단을 돌아 사우스 조지아 및 샌드위치 제도를 발견 (두 번째 항해)
하와이 제도를 방문한 최초의 유럽인이 됨 (세 번째 항해)
사망

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of Australia and New Zealand
keyAchievements.description: Was the first European to encounter the eastern coastline of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and completed the first recorded circumnavigation of New Zealand.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of accurate maps
keyAchievements.description: Produced maps of unprecedented accuracy with his excellent seamanship and surveying skills, changing the shape of the world map.

keyAchievements.title: Scurvy prevention
keyAchievements.description: Significantly reduced the incidence of scurvy among his crew by enforcing strict hygiene and providing diets including foods like sauerkraut.

keyAchievements.title: Search for the Northwest Passage and Antarctica
keyAchievements.description: Crossed the Antarctic Circle for the first time on his second voyage, and explored the Bering Strait in search of the Northwest Passage on his third voyage.

keyAchievements.title: 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 뉴펀들랜드와 뉴질랜드의 해도를 제작했으며, 특히 뉴펀들랜드 해안의 대규모의 정확한 지도를 처음으로 만들었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 괴혈병 사망자 없는 세계 일주
keyAchievements.description: 첫 번째 항해에서 사상 처음으로 괴혈병에 의한 사망자를 내지 않고 세계 일주를 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 영국 식민지 개척 기여
keyAchievements.description: 태평양을 남쪽 끝에서부터 북쪽 끝까지 탐험하여 영국의 식민지 개척에 크게 이바지했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 대항해시대의 종결
keyAchievements.description: 소위 대항해시대의 끝을 장식한 인물로, 그의 탐험을 전후하여 세계의 거의 모든 지역이 유럽인에게 알려지게 되었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 뉴질랜드 해안 지도 제작 및 해협 발견
keyAchievements.description: 뉴질랜드 해안의 거의 완벽한 지도를 제작하고 뉴질랜드 남북섬을 나누는 쿡 해협을 발견했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남극권 최초 돌입
keyAchievements.description: 유럽인으로서 처음으로 남극권에 돌입하는 위대한 업적을 달성했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 남방 대륙 탐험 종지부
keyAchievements.description: 남극 대륙을 발견하였지만, 남방 대륙이 인류가 거주 가능한 위도에는 존재하지 않는다는 것을 확인하여 전설의 남방 대륙 탐험에 종지부를 찍었습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 정확한 경도 결정
keyAchievements.description: 두 번째 항해에서 크로노미터가 활약하여 정확한 경도를 결정하는 데 크게 기여했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 북서부 해안 탐사 및 해도 제작
keyAchievements.description: 캘리포니아에서 베링 해협에 이르는 미국의 북서부 해안 대부분의 해도를 제작하고 알래스카의 곳곳을 파악했습니다.

keyAchievements.title: 세계 일주 항해 일지 작성
keyAchievements.description: 자필 원고로 세계 일주 항해 일지를 남겼습니다(제2 항해).

faq.question: What is Captain James Cook's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: Leading three Pacific voyages, discovering the eastern coast of Australia and the Hawaiian Islands, and mapping the Pacific Ocean in great detail.

faq.question: Did James Cook discover Australia?
faq.answer: He was not the first European to discover Australia. Dutch explorers had reached the western coast earlier, but Cook was the first to explore and map the eastern coast.

faq.question: How did Captain Cook die?
faq.answer: During his third voyage in 1779, after returning to Hawaii, a conflict arose with the indigenous people over a stolen boat, and he was killed during the altercation.

faq.question: Did Captain Cook find the Northwest Passage?
faq.answer: No, he explored up to the Bering Strait but could not find a navigable Northwest Passage because he was blocked by ice.

faq.question: What was Captain Cook's relationship with the indigenous people like?
faq.answer: He generally tried to maintain peaceful relations, but there were also cultural misunderstandings and violent clashes, a legacy that remains controversial today.

faq.question: 그의 첫 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 왕립 학회는 금성 일면통과 관측을 위해 그를 남태평양에 파견했습니다.

faq.question: 그의 두 번째 항해의 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 남방 대륙(테라 아우스트랄리스)을 발견하기 위해 왕립 학회에 의해 다시 파견되었습니다.

faq.question: 그의 세 번째 항해의 공식적인 목적은 무엇이었나요?
faq.answer: 북극해를 빠져나와 태평양과 대서양을 잇는 북서 항로를 탐색하는 것이었습니다.

timeline.event: Born in Hochheim (or Tambach), Thuringia.
Traveled to Paris to study.
Appointed Prior in Erfurt and Vicar of Thuringia.
Obtained a master's degree (Magister) at the University of Paris.
Appointed Provincial of the newly established province of Saxony.
Appointed Vicar General of the province of Bohemia.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of German Mysticism
keyAchievements.description: Known as the father of German mysticism, profoundly influencing later mystics such as Johannes Tauler and Heinrich Seuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the German Language
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role in the development of the German language by delivering sermons and writing treatises not only in Latin but also in Middle High German.

keyAchievements.title: Theology of the Birth of God in the Soul
keyAchievements.description: Emphasized that the divine spark (Fünklein) exists in the deepest part of the human soul, and that humans can achieve union with God there.

faq.question: Who was Meister Eckhart?
faq.answer: Meister Eckhart was a German Catholic priest, theologian, philosopher, and mystic belonging to the Dominican Order.

faq.question: What does 'Meister' mean?
faq.answer: 'Meister' is a title referring to the academic degree of Master in Theology he obtained in Paris, indicating his status as an authoritative teacher.

faq.question: Why was Meister Eckhart accused of heresy?
faq.answer: Some of his expressions emphasizing the union of God and the human soul were deemed radical by the church authorities of the time, leading to suspicions of pantheism or self-deification.

timeline.event: Born in Horsens, Denmark
Went to sea and engaged in voyages to the East Indies

keyAchievements.title: First Kamchatka Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Through the First Kamchatka Expedition, he confirmed that the Asian and North American continents were separated.

keyAchievements.title: Second Kamchatka Expedition
keyAchievements.description: Led the massive Second Kamchatka Expedition (Great Northern Expedition), charting the Arctic coast of Siberia and discovering the coast of Alaska and the Aleutian Islands.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration of the Bering Strait and Bering Sea
keyAchievements.description: Explored the strait and sea separating Asia and North America, which were named the Bering Strait and the Bering Sea in his honor.

faq.question: What is Vitus Bering's nationality?
faq.answer: He was born in Denmark but spent most of his life exploring as an officer in the Russian Navy.

faq.question: What did Vitus Bering discover during his first expedition?
faq.answer: He confirmed that the Asian and North American continents are not connected but separated by a strait.

faq.question: How did Vitus Bering die?
faq.answer: During his second expedition, he was shipwrecked on an uninhabited island on his return journey from Alaska and died of scurvy along with 28 of his crew members.

faq.question: What geographical features are named after Vitus Bering?
faq.answer: The Bering Strait, the Bering Sea, Bering Island, and the Bering Glacier are named after him.

timeline.event: Appointed to lead an expedition tasked by King John II of Portugal to find the southern tip of Africa
Sailed past the coast of Angola
Sailed past present-day Namibia
Caught in a massive storm off the coast of South Africa and drifted for 13 days
Landed at Mossel Bay after turning the Cape of Good Hope
Reached the Great Fish River (Rio do Infante) but was forced to turn back due to crew demands
Discovered the Cape of Good Hope on the return journey and named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas)

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Cape of Good Hope
keyAchievements.description: Became the first European to discover the Cape of Good Hope at the southern tip of Africa in 1488.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneered the sea route to India
keyAchievements.description: Proved that the Atlantic and Indian Oceans were connected, opening up the possibility of a direct sea route from Europe to Asia (India).

faq.question: What is Bartolomeu Dias's most famous achievement?
faq.answer: He was the first European to sail to the southern tip of Africa and discover the Cape of Good Hope.

faq.question: Why is the Cape of Good Hope called the Cape of Good Hope?
faq.answer: Dias originally named it the 'Cape of Storms' (Cabo das Tormentas), but King John II of Portugal renamed it the 'Cape of Good Hope' (Cabo da Boa Esperança), signifying the hope of reaching India.

faq.question: Did Bartolomeu Dias reach India?
faq.answer: No. He proved the possibility of a route to India, but had to turn back due to a lack of supplies and his crew's demands. It was Vasco da Gama who actually reached India later.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a vicar in Berkeley, Gloucestershire.
Became an apprentice to surgeon Daniel Ludlow in Sodbury.
Began studying medicine under John Hunter at St George's Hospital, University of London.
Became a general practitioner in his hometown.
Developed an interest in smallpox.
Gathered his observations on smallpox.
Married Catharine Kingscote.
Received his MD from the University of St Andrews in Scotland.
John Hunter died, ending their friendship.
Successfully performed the first vaccination on a local boy.
Reported the results of 23 experiments to the Royal Society and published his paper 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae'.
Received a grant from the government (part of a total of £30,000).
The Royal Jennerian Society was founded to promote vaccination in London.
Received the remainder of the grant from the government.
Made his last visit to London.
Died of a stroke in his hometown.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Cowpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the cowpox vaccine, earning him the title 'Father of Immunology'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined the Term Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Coined the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

keyAchievements.title: Development and Distribution of Smallpox Vaccine
keyAchievements.description: Successfully performed the first vaccination on a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine and contributing to its worldwide distribution.

faq.question: Who is Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was an English physician, the discoverer of the cowpox vaccine, and the father of immunology.

faq.question: When was Edward Jenner born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was born on May 17, 1749, and died on January 26, 1823.

faq.question: What led Edward Jenner to coin the term vaccine?
faq.answer: Jenner created the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from 'vacca', the Latin word for cow.

faq.question: What are the main achievements of Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner discovered the cowpox vaccine and successfully administered the first vaccination to a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine.

timeline.event: December 10: Emily Elizabeth Dickinson was born in Amherst, Massachusetts, USA.
May 15: Died at the age of fifty-five.

keyAchievements.title: Poetry Creation
keyAchievements.description: Wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

keyAchievements.title: Main Themes of Her Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Created poems with main themes including love, death, parting, the soul, and heaven.

keyAchievements.title: Lack of Public Recognition in Life
keyAchievements.description: Was largely unrecognized by the public during her lifetime, with only about 4 of her poems published in collections.

keyAchievements.title: Literary Exchanges in Life
keyAchievements.description: Consistently exchanged poems through correspondence with a few poets and intellectuals who recognized her talent during her lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Recognition of Genius
keyAchievements.description: Her genius was widely recognized after her death when her younger sister, Lavinia Norcross Dickinson, published her collections of poems.

keyAchievements.title: Musical Reinterpretation of Poetry
keyAchievements.description: Her poems have been set to music by renowned composers such as Aaron Copland.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson recognized by the public during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not recognized by the public during her lifetime, and only 4 of her poems were published.

faq.question: What themes did Emily Dickinson's poetry primarily deal with?
faq.answer: Her poetry mainly dealt with themes of love, death, separation, the soul, and heaven.

faq.question: How many poems did Emily Dickinson write?
faq.answer: She wrote nearly 2,000 poems.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson a reclusive poet?
faq.answer: She is known as a reclusive poet who strictly refrained from going out, but as a child, she had a lively personality, going out to the fields every day and playing with neighborhood children.

timeline.event: Born in Fuendetodos, Aragon, Spain.
Goya's family bought a house in Zaragoza and moved there a few years later.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected.
Was deeply moved by the works of Velázquez and Rembrandt in Madrid.
Submitted an application to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected again.
Traveled to Italy and won second prize at a painting exhibition in Parma; returned to Zaragoza later that year to become a painter for the Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar.
Married Josefa, Bayeu's sister.
Painted the portrait of the Count of Floridablanca, a close associate of Charles III.
Began painting portraits of Charles III.
Charles III died.
Charles IV ascended to the throne, and Goya became a court painter.
Contracted cholera and lost his hearing due to a high fever.
Painted a series of fantastical works.
Painted the frescoes of the Royal Chapel of St. Anthony of La Florida in Madrid.
Published the print series 'Los Caprichos' and became the First Court Painter.
Painted 'Charles IV of Spain and His Family' and 'The Nude Maja'.
Painted 'The Clothed Maja'.
The Peninsular War broke out between France and Spain, and Goya left documentary paintings of it.
Created the print series 'The Disasters of War'.
His wife Josefa died.
The Spanish Inquisition confiscated the Maja series, deeming them obscene.
The French army retreated, and Ferdinand VII returned to the throne.
Moved to Bordeaux, France.
Returned to Spain but went back to Bordeaux due to deteriorating health.
Died at the age of 82.

keyAchievements.title: Status as a Romantic Painter and Printmaker
keyAchievements.description: Spain's representative Romantic painter and printmaker, known as the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting and a genius artist who foreshadowed Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Court Painter
keyAchievements.description: Became a court painter in 1789 and the First Court Painter in 1799, painting portraits of the king, queen, and many royals and nobles.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the Print Series 'Los Caprichos'
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Los Caprichos' in 1799, created using aquatint, to expose the reality and superstitions of the corrupt Catholic Church.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of War Documentary Paintings and Prints
keyAchievements.description: Left documentary paintings of the Peninsular War, 'The Second of May 1808' and 'The Third of May 1808', and created the print series 'The Disasters of War' depicting the horrors of war.

keyAchievements.title: 'Maja' Series
keyAchievements.description: Painted 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja', with 'The Nude Maja' being considered the 'first life-size female nude in Western art'.

keyAchievements.title: Black Paintings Series
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, at the 'Quinta del Sordo' (House of the Deaf Man), he created the 'Black Paintings' series depicting madness hidden behind reason, famously including 'Saturn Devouring His Son'.

faq.question: How is Francisco Goya evaluated as a painter?
faq.answer: He is evaluated as Spain's leading Romantic painter and printmaker, the representative of 18th-century Spanish painting, and a genius who heralded the beginning of Impressionism.

faq.question: When did Goya lose his hearing?
faq.answer: He lost his hearing in 1792 due to a high fever after contracting cholera.

faq.question: What are Goya's major print series?
faq.answer: His major print series include 'Los Caprichos', published in 1799, and 'The Disasters of War', created between 1810 and 1820.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Goya's 'Maja' series?
faq.answer: 'The Nude Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja' portray the same woman in the identical pose, and are evaluated as 'the first life-size female nudes in Western art'.

faq.question: What was the trend of Goya's works in his later years?
faq.answer: After losing his hearing in 1792, his paintings became darker; following the Peninsular War, he painted works with heavy themes and dark tones; and in his later years, he left the 'Black Paintings' series at the 'House of the Deaf Man'.

timeline.event: Born at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.
Studied at the Reading Abbey Girls' School in Reading, Berkshire.
Started writing novels at the age of 14.
Wrote the epistolary novel 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility' based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father relinquished his rectory and the family moved to Bath, Somerset.
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it the next day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crosby & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a cottage in Chawton with the help of her third brother, Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Dedicated 'Emma' to the Prince Regent (later George IV) shortly before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, leaving her bedridden for long periods.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 while writing 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Began Writing Novels
keyAchievements.description: Started writing stories at the age of 14 and grew as a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original draft was titled 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and Dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV) and published in December 1815.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the 'Great Tradition' of the English Novel
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as having founded the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel by addressing the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

keyAchievements.title: Socially Critical Realism Literature
keyAchievements.description: Created a sophisticated literary world based on realism, satirizing the material-oriented trends and hypocrisy of contemporary society with keen observation and a sharp critical perspective.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous Literary Re-evaluation and Pop Culture Influence
keyAchievements.description: Elevated to the literary canon through critics' acclaim from the late 19th century onwards, and in the late 20th century, her works became a popular cult phenomenon, being adapted into films and plays.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, at the rectory in Steventon, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: Her works include 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816).

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Jane Austen's novels?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by depicting the lives of upper and middle-class women in 18th-century England with a delicate gaze and witty style.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but in the 20th century, her works gained massive popularity and were adapted into films multiple times.

faq.question: Did Jane Austen ever marry?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy, but it did not lead to marriage. She received a proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither in 1802 but rejected it the next day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in Pentonville, London, England.
Began learning Greek.
Began learning Latin, Euclid, and algebra, and acted as a tutor for his younger siblings.
Reached the level of being able to read original texts of Plato and Demosthenes.
Started studying scholastic logic and original texts of Aristotle's logic.
Began studying political economy.
Stayed in France for a year and took courses in chemistry, zoology, and logic at the Faculty of Sciences in Montpellier.
Suffered from a nervous breakdown.
Founded the Utilitarian Society with his friends.
Published 'A System of Logic'.
Published 'Essays on Some Unsettled Questions of Political Economy'.
Published 'Principles of Political Economy'.
Married Harriet Taylor.
Harriet Taylor died, and he retired from the East India Company.
Published 'On Liberty'.
Published 'Utilitarianism' and 'Considerations on Representative Government'.
Elected as Lord Rector of the University of St Andrews and elected to the House of Commons as a Member of Parliament for the City of Westminster.
Term as Member of Parliament ended.
Became the first person in Parliament to advocate for women's right to vote, and published 'The Subjection of Women'.
Died in Avignon, France, and 'Autobiography' was published.
Published 'Three Essays on Religion'.
Published 'Chapters on Socialism'.

keyAchievements.title: Completion of the System of Logic
keyAchievements.description: Published 'A System of Logic' in 1843, completing Bacon's inductive logic and presenting a groundbreaking methodology for natural science research.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Liberal Political Theory
keyAchievements.description: Left behind a classic work on the concept of liberty through 'On Liberty', presenting the basic principles of modern liberal democracy by defending freedom of expression as a fundamental right of citizens.

keyAchievements.title: Development of Utilitarianism
keyAchievements.description: Developed Bentham's utilitarianism, his godfather and teacher, into his own system by adding Saint-Simonianism and Romanticism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for Women's Rights and Social Reform
keyAchievements.description: As a Member of Parliament, he advocated for various social reforms including women's rights, proportional representation, labor unions, and cooperative organization of agriculture, and was the first to argue for women's suffrage in Parliament.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of Market Socialism Theory
keyAchievements.description: Considered one of the early theorists of market socialism for advocating the replacement of capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives.

faq.question: How was John Stuart Mill educated?
faq.answer: He was educated primarily by his father, James Mill, and was a precocious child, learning Greek at the age of three and Latin and advanced mathematics at eight.

faq.question: What were Mill's views on religion?
faq.answer: He was critical of religion itself, never having had religious beliefs, denied Christian doctrines, and viewed religion as the greatest enemy of freedom.

faq.question: How did Harriet Taylor influence John Stuart Mill?
faq.answer: Harriet profoundly influenced Mill's thoughts, particularly strengthening his advocacy for women's rights, and influenced the writing of 'On Liberty' and 'The Subjection of Women'.

faq.question: What did John Stuart Mill think about the 'stationary state'?
faq.answer: He believed that a stationary state of capital and wealth might not be terrible but could actually be a much more advanced and happier state than the present, anticipating that humanity would be less concerned with money and focus more on values like dignity, integrity, and justice.

faq.question: Was John Stuart Mill a socialist?
faq.answer: He called himself a socialist and is considered one of the early market socialist theorists for advocating the replacement of capitalist businesses with worker cooperatives.

timeline.event: Henrik Johan Ibsen was born in Skien, Norway.
At age 18, had a son, Hans Jacob Hendrichsen Birkedalen, from a relationship with Else Sophie Jensdatter Birkedalen.
Published his first play, 'Catiline', under the pseudonym 'Brynjolf Bjarme', and had his first stage production, 'The Burial Mound'.
Became the artistic director of the Christiania Theater and married Suzannah Thoresen.
Son Sigurd was born.
Left Christiania for Sorrento, Italy, beginning 27 years of living abroad.
Published the play 'Brand', which brought him critical acclaim and financial success.
Published the play 'Peer Gynt', for which Edvard Grieg composed the incidental music.
Moved from Italy to Dresden, Germany.
Published his only collection of poetry, 'Poems' (Digte).
Wrote 'Emperor and Galilean', dramatizing the life of the Roman Emperor Julian the Apostate, and was knighted.
Moved to Munich and began work on his first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society'.
His first modern realistic drama, 'The Pillars of Society', was published and first performed.
Published the play 'A Doll's House', which brought him international recognition.
Published the play 'Ghosts', containing commentary on the morality of Ibsen's society.
Published the play 'An Enemy of the People', addressing controversial themes.
Published the play 'The Wild Duck', considered by many to be Ibsen's finest work.
Published the later work 'Hedda Gabler', exploring psychological conflict.
